About This Project

— ✦ —

About This Project

The Shakespeare Digital Variorum

Scholarly Tradition · Artificial Intelligence · Universal Access

✶ ❧ ✶

The New Variorum Shakespeare editions have been the gold standard of Shakespearean scholarship for over a century, bringing together the most important critical commentary, textual analysis, and historical context in comprehensive annotated volumes. The Shakespeare Digital Variorum builds on this tradition, harnessing the power of artificial intelligence to make centuries of scholarly insight accessible to everyone — from high school students to veteran researchers.

Our platform integrates authoritative texts based on the Folger Shakespeare Library editions of each play, with commentary from major variorum editions, scholarly databases, and historical sources. We include references to film adaptations, stage productions, and cultural interpretations that show how Shakespeare’s works have lived and evolved across different media and eras. Using advanced AI, we can instantly provide plain-language explanations of complex passages, trace the etymology of Early Modern English words as they appear in context, analyze prosodic patterns across scenes and plays, and illuminate the historical events and cultural forces that shaped each work.

✶

Radical Accessibility

The goal is radical accessibility. Whether you’re encountering Hamlet’s soliloquies for the first time or you’re a seasoned scholar investigating textual variants, the Shakespeare Digital Variorum meets you where you are. Students can receive immediate clarification of archaic language and historical references. Researchers can discover thematic connections across the complete canon. Theater practitioners can access centuries of performance history, from Garrick to Branagh to contemporary productions worldwide.

We believe Shakespeare belongs to everyone, not just academics. By connecting scholarly commentary with film clips, performance videos, and multimedia resources, we show how these 400-year-old plays continue to speak to modern audiences. A teenager reading Romeo and Juliet can instantly see how Baz Luhrmann reimagined the balcony scene, while a graduate student can access the full textual apparatus from multiple critical editions.

This isn’t about replacing human scholarship but about democratizing it. Every AI-generated insight is grounded in the work of generations of Shakespeare scholars, from the earliest editors to contemporary critics. We’ve simply made it possible for anyone to access that collective wisdom instantly, searchably, and in conversation with the texts themselves.

✶

New Variorum Coverage

The pre-1956 New Variorum Shakespeare series includes 23 plays out of copyright, along with 2 non-dramatic poetry volumes. This site presents the complete NVS scholarly notes for 20 of those 23 plays — all volumes that were available in full text. Three plays are forthcoming as access permits.

Three Plays Forthcoming

The following three volumes were accessible only as Google Books snippets and could not be fully processed. They will be added as access permits.

  • Henry IV, Part 2 (1940, ed. M. A. Shaaber)
  • Troilus and Cressida (1953, ed. H. N. Hillebrand & T. W. Baldwin)
  • Richard II (1955, ed. M. W. Black)

Non-Dramatic Volumes

  • The Poems (1938, ed. H. E. Rollins)
  • The Sonnets (1944, ed. H. E. Rollins, 2 vols.)

Summary

✶

The Next Evolution

The Shakespeare Digital Variorum represents the next evolution of the variorum tradition: comprehensive, scholarly, and designed for universal access. Shakespeare’s world is vast — and now everyone can explore all of it.

— ✦ ❧ ✦ —

Brad

Add content for this panel here.

What's New Variorum?

The New Variorum Shakespeare is one of the most comprehensive scholarly editions of Shakespeare's works ever created. Begun in 1871 by Philadelphia lawyer Horace Howard Furness, this monumental project aimed to document every change that centuries of editors had made to Shakespeare's texts, along with everything editors and commentators had written about them—all in one place. The term "variorum" comes from the Latin editio cum notis variorum, meaning "an edition with the notes of various editors and commentators."

Furness devoted the rest of his life to this herculean task, personally completing 18 volumes between 1871 and his death in 1912. His first volume, Romeo and Juliet, appeared in 1871, followed by Macbeth (1873), Hamlet (two volumes, 1877), King Lear (1880), and others through The Tempest (1892) and Twelfth Night (1901). His son, Horace Howard Furness Jr., continued the work, and after 1936, the Modern Language Association took over the project.

Each Furness variorum volume is a massive scholarly apparatus surrounding Shakespeare's text. For any given line or word, it presents textual variants from different early editions, editorial commentary spanning from the 18th century through the early 20th century, glosses of difficult passages, critical interpretations showing how different scholars understood disputed passages, performance history, and source materials Shakespeare drew upon.

The Digital Revolution

This website gives you unprecedented access to the original Furness variorum editions—those dense, massive volumes that were traditionally found only in research libraries and could weigh up to 30 pounds each. Through advanced RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) technology, we've digitized and indexed Furness's extraordinary achievement, making centuries of accumulated Shakespeare scholarship instantly accessible at the click of a button.

When you click on any line or word, our system intelligently retrieves the exact relevant commentary from these historic Furness volumes. That obscure phrase that's puzzled you? There's probably a Victorian scholar who spent years thinking about it. That textual crux you're wondering about? You can instantly see how editors from 1733 to 1912 tried to solve it.

A Note on the Modern New Variorum Project

While our website focuses on the original Furness editions with their unparalleled historical depth, there is exciting new work happening in variorum scholarship. Since 2019, Texas A&M University, in partnership with the Modern Language Association, has been creating entirely new digital variorum editions that update and expand Furness's work for the 21st century. These new editions, which incorporate modern scholarship and cutting-edge digital tools, are available at newvariorumshakespeare.org. However, the commentary you're accessing here comes exclusively from the original Furness volumes—giving you direct access to the foundational scholarship that has shaped how we understand Shakespeare for over a century.

What we've accomplished is transforming Furness's life's work into an immediate, interactive experience. You're not just reading Shakespeare—you're joining a centuries-long conversation about every single word, with the greatest Shakespeare scholars in history as your guides, all powered by cutting-edge AI technology that brings their insights directly to you.

Who are the New Variorum Critics?

Critics Cited in the New Variorum Edition of Macbeth

Edited by Horace Howard Furness (1873)

This document provides a comprehensive list of all critics, commentators, editors, scholars, translators, actors, and other persons cited in the New Variorum Edition of Shakespeare's Macbeth. Where the volume's "List of Books" (pp. 547–555) identifies the specific work from which annotations are drawn, that bibliographic information is included.

I. Editors Collated in the Textual Notes

These are the editions systematically collated in the textual apparatus, as listed on pp. 545–546 of the volume.

III. Actors and Theatre Figures Discussed

The revised edition includes an account of actors from Garrick to the early 1900s. The following performers and theatre figures are discussed in the volume.

Bernhardt, Sarah (Madame) — Actress

Betterton, Thomas — Actor, associated with D'Avenant's version (1670s)

Booth, Edwin — American tragedian; prompt-book edited by W. Winter (1878)

Calcraft, Mr. — Theatre manager

Darling, Miss — Actress

Forbes-Robertson, J. — Actor

Garrick, David — Actor; extensively discussed in Actors appendix

Irving, Sir Henry — Actor-manager, Lyceum Theatre; contributed critical essays

Kean, Charles — Actor/producer

Kemble, John Philip — Actor; author of Macbeth Reconsidered (1786)

Kemble, Mrs. F. A. (Fanny) — Actress; wrote on characters in Macbeth (1867)

Lacy — Actor (painted in Charles II's time)

Macklin, Charles — Actor; innovator in costume

Macready, William Charles — Actor; Reminiscences cited

Martin, Lady (Helena Faucit) — Actress; Some of Shakespeare's Female Characters (1891)

Melvin, Mr. — Actor, Covent Garden (1806–7)

Phelps, Samuel — Actor/producer

Pritchard, Mrs. Hannah — Actress

Sheridan, Richard Brinsley — Theatre manager

Siddons, Mrs. Sarah — Actress; her Lady Macbeth extensively discussed

IV. Historical Sources, Chroniclers, and Dramatists

Bellenden, John — Translator of Boece's chronicles

Boece (Boethius), Hector — Scottish historian; source material for Holinshed

Buchanan, George — Scottish historian

Burton, Robert — Anatomy of Melancholy

D'Avenant, Sir William — Adapted Macbeth (1673 and 1674 versions reprinted in appendix)

Fordun, John of — Scottish chronicler

Guthrie, William — Scottish historian

Gwynne, Dr. Mathew — Author of Latin interlude performed at Oxford before James I

Heylin, Peter — Cosmography (source for D'Avenant's 'Argument')

Holinshed, Raphael — Chronicles (1587) — primary source; passages reprinted in appendix

Middleton, Thomas — The Witch (scenes reprinted in appendix); Works (ed. Dyce, 1843)

Nixon, Anthony — The Oxford Triumph (1605)

Scot, Reginald — Discoverie of Witchcraft (1584)

Scott, Sir Walter — Miscellaneous Essays; historical discussions

Skene, W. F. — Scottish historian (costume)

Tighernach — Irish chronicler

Topsell, E. — History of Serpents (London, 1608)

Weyer, Johann — Demonologist

Wintoun, Andrew of — Scottish chronicler

V. Other Persons Referenced

Additional literary figures, musicians, artists, and others referenced in the volume but not primarily as Shakespearean critics.

Addison, Joseph — Dramatist (Cato)

Byron, Lord — Poet; influence discussed

Chappell — Music historian (Music appendix)

Defoe, Daniel — Referenced for costume

Dryden, John — Poet/dramatist

Fitzgerald, Edward — Translator

Ford, John — Dramatist

Grimm, Jacob — Folklorist

Heywood, Thomas — Dramatist

Kyd, Thomas — Dramatist; Spanish Tragedy parallels discussed

Locke, Matthew — Composer of music for D'Avenant's Macbeth

Lowell, James Russell — American critic

Massinger, Philip — Dramatist

Milton, John — Poet (contemplated Macbeth as subject)

Nashe, Thomas — Terrors of the Night

Retzsch, Moritz — Artist (Outlines)

Southey, Robert — Poet

Spenser, Edmund — Poet

Swinburne, A. C. — Literary critic

Thomas, L. F. — Proof-reader acknowledged in preface

Waldron, F. G. — Dramatic editor; identified Hunter as author of Harry Rowe's Macbeth

Wister, Mrs. A. L. — Translated German passages for the edition

How To Use

Learn about the New Variorum editions that power this site.

Selecting Text for Analysis:

  • Click any line in the play to select it (highlighted). Click additional lines to add them to your selection.
  • Once you've selected lines, an Analyze Text button will appear on the right panel.
  • To deselect a single line, click it again. To clear all selections, press Escape or click the Clear Selection button.

To use this site, begin by selecting any lines of the play. Each selection opens three levels of analysis:

  • Basic provides a plain-language paraphrase and a straightforward explanation of the text.
  • Expert offers a more detailed discussion.
  • Full Fathom Five expands further, including extensive commentary and the notes from the New Variorum editions published between the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

Not every play has a New Variorum edition; only 23 volumes were completed before the project was abandoned in the mid-20th century (and before Texas A&M picked up the project again in recent years). Nevertheless, the series is widely regarded as the gold standard of Shakespeare scholarship, compiling centuries of commentary and editorial insight from the 17th century onward. For the first time, this site makes the New Variorum materials available online, opening a vast body of critical tradition to readers outside the library stacks.

Beyond the written annotations, the site also connects each line to its broader cultural life. Buttons alongside the text may bring up film scene drawn from YouTube's extensive catalog of Shakespeare production that dramatize the very line you have selected. Other buttons link to JSTOR articles and additional scholarly resources on the Internet, offering direct access to research that situates the passage in its academic and historical context.

Taken together, these features transform each line into a gateway: to the play's language, its centuries of interpretation, and its continuing presence on stage and screen.

How To Help

How To Help

Shakespeare Digital Variorum

This site is attempting to make accessible more than 10,000 pages of the 20 New Variorum Shakespeare volumes we have processed — dense, double-columned pages containing centuries’ worth of notes drawn from multi-lingual sources and curated with the attention of a forensic accountant. Across those volumes, editors like Horace Howard Furness and his scholarly successors tracked every significant emendation, variant reading, and critical comment produced by generations of editors working in English, Latin, German, French, and beyond.

We are quite sure that mistakes have crept into our work — whether by omission, mistranscription, or the ordinary failures of automated processing applied to nineteenth-century typography. A misread word in a footnote, a dropped citation, a garbled German gloss: these are the kinds of errors that no algorithm catches and no single reader finds. Only the collective attention of many readers, over time, will surface them.

What to Look For

If you are reading a play and notice that a commentary note seems truncated, misattributed, or simply wrong — that a quotation from a critic has been mangled, that a textual variant has gone missing, or that a passage in the original Variorum volume does not match what appears here — we want to know. Corrections of any size are welcome, from a transposed date to a missing paragraph. The original volumes, many of which are freely available on HathiTrust and the Internet Archive, are the authoritative source against which our text can always be checked.

Get in Touch

We welcome reports from anyone — students, scholars, enthusiasts, and casual readers alike. There is no error too small to mention and no expertise required to spot one. Please write to us describing what you found, which play and passage it concerns, and, if possible, what the correct text should be.

You can reach the project team at: help@newvariorum.org. We read every message and will acknowledge corrections as we incorporate them. If you would like attribution for a correction, please say so and we will be glad to include your name in the acknowledgments.

Who are the NV Critics for Antony and Cleopatra?

Critics Cited in the New Variorum Edition of Antony and Cleopatra

Edited by Horace Howard Furness

This document provides a comprehensive list of all critics, commentators, editors, scholars, translators, actors, and other persons cited in the New Variorum Edition of Shakespeare's Antony and Cleopatra. Where the volume's "List of Books" identifies the specific work from which annotations are drawn, that bibliographic information is included.

Who are the NV Critics for Coriolanus?

Critics Cited in the New Variorum Edition of Coriolanus

Edited by Horace Howard Furness, Jr., A.B., Litt.D.

This document provides a comprehensive list of all critics, commentators, editors, scholars, translators, actors, and other persons cited in the New Variorum Edition of Shakespeare's Coriolanus. Where the volume's "List of Books" identifies the specific work from which annotations are drawn, that bibliographic information is included.

Baker, G. P.: Development of Shakespeare as a Dramatist — New York, 1907

Baynes, T. S.: Shakespeare Studies and Other Essays — London

Beeching, H. C.: The Character of Shakespeare (British Academy Lecture) — London, 1917

Beisly, Sidney: Shakespeare's Garden — London, 1864

Birch, W. J.: Inquiry Into the Religion and Philosophy of Shakespeare — London, 1848

Boas, F. S.: Shakespeare and His Predecessors — London, 1896

Bodenstedt, F.: Shakespeare's Frauengestalten; Frauencharaktere — Dresden, 1874

Bradley, A. C.: Shakespearean Tragedy — London, 1905

Bradley, A. C.: Coriolanus (British Academy Lecture) — London, 1912

Brandes, Georg: William Shakespeare — London, 1900

Brink, B. ten: Five Lectures on Shakespeare (trans. Julia Franklin) — New York, 1895

Brooke, C. F. Tucker: Shakespeare's Plutarch — London, 1909

III. Actors and Theatre Figures

Burbage, Richard — First known actor of Coriolanus (died 1619)

Quin — Possibly first Coriolanus at Lincoln's Inn Fields, 1720

Mossop — Coriolanus at Drury Lane, 1754

Sheridan, Thomas — Coriolanus at Covent Garden, 1754; also adapter

Woffington, Mrs. — Veturia, Covent Garden, 1754

Kemble, John Philip — Coriolanus, Drury Lane, 1789 ff.; greatest impersonation

Siddons, Mrs. — Volumnia opposite Kemble; legendary interpretation

Cooke, George Frederick — Coriolanus, Drury Lane, 1804

Young, Charles Mayne — Coriolanus, 1813

Macready, William Charles — Coriolanus, first time 1819; retained to 1851

Kean, Edmund — Coriolanus, Drury Lane, 1820

Phelps, Samuel — Coriolanus, Sadler's Wells, 1848 ff.

Glyn, Miss — Volumnia, Sadler's Wells, 1848

Forrest, Edwin — Coriolanus, New York, 1832 ff.; major American interpreter

Cushman, Charlotte — Volumnia opposite Forrest, 1838

Salvini, Tommaso — Coriolanus, Metropolitan Opera House, New York, 1886

Benson, R. F. — Coriolanus, Comedy Theatre, London, 1901

Ward, Genevieve — Volumnia opposite Benson, 1901

Irving, Sir Henry — Coriolanus, Lyceum Theatre, 1901 (last Shakespearean role)

Terry, Ellen — Volumnia, Lyceum, 1901

Anderson, J. — Coriolanus, various London theatres, 1851–53; New York, 1844

McCullough, John — Cominius/Coriolanus, New York, 1863, 1878

Barnay, L. — Coriolanus, Bowery Theatre, 1883

IV. Historical Sources, Chroniclers, and Dramatists

Plutarch — Primary source: Lives of the Noble Grecians and Romans

North, Sir Thomas — Translator of Plutarch (via Amyot); Shakespeare's direct source

Amyot, Jacques — French translator of Plutarch; North translated from his version

Livy — Roman historian; alternative source for Thomson's Coriolanus

Dionysius Halicarnassensis — Greek historian of Rome; cited for Coriolanus legend

Tate, Nahum — The Ingratitude of a Commonwealth (1682 adaptation)

Dennis, John — The Invader of his Country (1719 adaptation)

Thomson, James — Coriolanus (independent tragedy, 1747/1749)

Sheridan, Thomas — Coriolanus; or, The Roman Matron (1785 Shakespeare-Thomson adaptation)

Hardy, Alexandre — Coriolan (French, 1625; independent of Shakespeare)

Calderón — Las Armas de la Hermosura (Spanish dramatic fantasia)

Collin, Heinrich von — Coriolan (German tragedy, 1802/1804)

Beethoven — Overture to Collin's Coriolan, Opus 62 (1807)

V. Other Persons Referenced

Furness, Horace Howard, Jr. — Editor of this New Variorum edition

Sothern, Edward Hugh and Julia Marlowe — Dedicatees

Niebuhr — Roman historian; Coriolanus as legendary

Mommsen, Theodor — History of Rome; cited on tribunes

Boaden, James — Biographer of Kemble and Siddons

Schmitz, L. Dora — Translator of Ulrici

Bunnett, Miss F. E. — Translator of Gervinus

Black, John — Translator of Schlegel's Lectures

Franklin, Julia — Translator of ten Brink

Ainslie, Douglas — Translator of Croce

Bradley, A. C. — Coriolanus British Academy Lecture (1912)

MacCallum, M. W. — Shakespeare's Roman Plays and Their Background (1910)

Swinburne, A. C. — A Study of Shakespeare

Wagner, Richard — Essay on Beethoven (Beethoven/Shakespeare theme)

Alma Tadema — Scenery/costume designer for Irving's 1901 revival

Mackenzie, Sir A. C. — Composed music for Irving's 1901 revival

Odell, George C. D. — Annals of the New York Stage; Shakespeare from Betterton to Irving

Winter, William — Shakespeare on the Stage (primary theatre source)

Who are the NV Critics for Cymbeline?

Edited by Horace Howard Furness (completed posthumously by H. H. Furness, Jr.)

This document provides a comprehensive list of all critics, commentators, editors, scholars, translators, actors, and other persons cited in the New Variorum Edition of Shakespeare's Cymbeline. Where the volume's "List of Books" identifies the specific work from which annotations are drawn, that bibliographic information is included.

Johnson, Dr. Samuel [Johns.] 1765

Malone, Edmond [Mal.] 1790

Boswell's Malone [Var. '21] 1821

The following lists all works from which annotations, commentary, and critical excerpts are drawn. An asterisk (*) marks books added for a revised edition where applicable.

Abbott, E. A.: Shakespearian Grammar — London, 1870

Bathurst, C.: On metrical tests — 1857

Birch, W. J.: Inquiry into the Religion and Philosophy of Shakespeare — London, 1848

Boccaccio, Giovanni: Decameron (Day 2, Novel 9) — Iachimo/wager plot source

Boodle, R. W.: On Source of Plot, The Rare Triumphs of Love and Fortune (N. & Q.) — 1887

Boswell-Stone, W. G.: Shakespeare's Holinshed

Bowden, H. S.: The Religion of Shakespeare

Brandl, Alois: Shakspere (on Source of Plot)

Campbell, T.: Dramatic Works of Shakespeare

Capell, Edward: Notes, etc. — London, 1779

Chalmers, George: Supplemental Apology

Child, F. J.: English and Scottish Ballads

Clarke, Charles Cowden: Shakespeare Characters — London, 1863

Clarke, C. and M. Cowden: The Shakespeare Key — London, 1879

Coleridge, Hartley: Criticism on Cymbeline

Coleridge, S. T.: Classification of Shakespeare's Plays

Collier, J. P.: New Particulars, etc.; Farther Particulars, etc.; Annals of the Stage — 1836, 1839

Cotgrave, R.: Dictionarie of the French and English Tongues

Courthope, W. J.: History of English Poetry, vol. IV

Daniel, P. A.: New Shakespeare Society Transactions — 1877–79

Drake, Nathan: Shakespeare and His Times

Edwards, Thomas: Canons of Criticism

Fleay, F. G.: Life and Work of Shakespeare; Chronicle of the English Drama; verse-tests — 1886, 1891

Fletcher, George: Studies of Shakespeare

Florio, John: Montaigne translation; Italian-English dictionary

Furnivall, F. J.: On metrical tests; Essay on Child-Marriages (E.E.T. Soc.) — 1874, 1897

Garnett, Dr. Richard: Essay on date (Shakespeare Jahrbuch) — 1901

Genée, R.: On German dramatic treatment of plot — Hildburghausen

Gervinus, G. G.: Shakespeare Commentaries (trans. by F. E. Bunnett)

Halliwell (-Phillipps), J. O.: Introduction to Cymbeline; Source of Plot

Hazlitt, William: Characters of Shakespeare's Plays; Lectures on Dram. Lit. — 1817; 1840

Heath, Benjamin: Revisal of Shakespeare's Text

Hertzberg, Dr. W.: Translation and Introduction (Deutsche Shakespeare-Gesellschaft) — Bremen, 1871

Holinshed, Raphael: Chronicles (British history, Cunobelinus, Hay story)

Hugo, François-Victor: Oeuvres complètes de Shakespeare

Hunter, Joseph: Criticism on date of Cymbeline

Ingleby, C. M.: On Date of Composition

Jameson, Mrs. Anna: Characteristics of Women / Shakespeare's Heroines

Knight, Charles: Pictorial Shakespeare (edition and commentary)

Langbaine, Gerard: An Account of the English Dramatick Poets — 1691

Lennox, Mrs. Charlotte: Shakespeare Illustrated

Leonhardt, Dr. B.: Relationship between Philaster and Hamlet and Cymbeline (Anglia) — 1883, 1885

Lettsom, W. N.: Conjectural emendations (ap. Walker)

Levy, S.: On Source of Plot (Anglia) — 1884

Lloyd, W. W.: Critical essay in Singer's edition

Martin, Lady (Helena Faucit): On Some of Shakespeare's Female Characters

Mason, J. Monck: Comments on the Last Edition of Shakespeare's Plays

Michel, Mons. Francisque: Editor of Miracle de Nostre-Dame and La Violette — Paris, 1839

Minsheu, John: Guide Into the Tongues — 1617

Murray, James A. H. (ed.): New English Dictionary (NED) — Oxford

Nares, R.: Glossary

Ohle, Dr. R.: On Source of Plot, French versions

Painter, William: Palace of Pleasure (The Lady of Boeme, from Bandello) — 1567

Payne, John (translator): Translation of Boccaccio's Decameron (Villon Society) — 1836

Raleigh, Walter: Shakespeare — 1909

Reich, H.: On Source of Plot, The Golden Ass (Jahrbuch) — 1905

Richardson, William: On Imogen (philosophical analysis)

Ritson, Joseph: Emendations and metrical observations

Roderick, Richard: Remarks on Henry VIII versification (in Edwards's Canons)

Schenkl, Karl: On Source of Plot, Sneewitchen (Germania) — Wien, 1864

Schlegel, A. W.: Lectures on Dramatic Art and Literature

Schmidt, Alexander: Shakespeare-Lexicon

Sidney, Sir Philip: Arcadia

Simrock, Karl: Die Quellen des Shakespeare

Simpson, Percy: Shakespearian Punctuation

Snider, D. J.: The Shakespearian Drama

Spedding, James: Proved joint authorship of Henry VIII (Sh. Soc. Trans.) — 1874

Swinburne, A. C.: A Study of Shakespeare

Thorndike, Dr. Ashley H.: The Influence of Beaumont and Fletcher on Shakespeare — 1901

Tieck, Ludwig: On Cymbeline (cited by Knight)

Tyrwhitt, Thomas: Emendations cited in Variorum editions

Ulrici, Hermann: Shakespeare's Dramatic Art (Bohn's ed.)

Walker, W. Sidney: Critical Examination of the Text of Shakespeare

Weichberger, K.: On Source of Plot

Wendell, Barrett: William Shakspere: A Study in Elizabethan Literature

Wolff: On correspondence between The Miracle and Cymbeline

Yardley, E.: On Source of Plot, Calderón connection (N. & Q.) — 1889

III. Actors and Theatre Figures

Faucit, Helena (Lady Martin) — Actress of Imogen; wrote On Some of Shakespeare's Female Characters

Durfey, Thomas — Author of The Injured Princess (1682 adaptation)

Herbert, Sir Henry — Master of the Revels; record of Cymbeline acted at Court, 1 Jan 1633

IV. Historical Sources, Chroniclers, and Dramatists

Holinshed, Raphael — Chronicles (British history, Cunobelinus, Hay story)

Boccaccio, Giovanni — Decameron, Day 2, Novel 9 (Iachimo/wager plot)

Le Roman de la Violette — Medieval French poem (birthmark/wager plot)

Le Roman du Comte de Poitiers — Medieval French poem (wager plot variant)

Un Miracle de Nostre-Dame — Medieval French religious drama (Otho/Denise plot)

Westward for Smelts — English tale (wager plot variant)

The Rare Triumphs of Love and Fortune — Anonymous English play (c. 1589)

Painter, William — Palace of Pleasure — The Lady of Boeme (from Bandello)

Bandello, Matteo — Nineteenth Story (via Painter)

Apuleius — The Golden Ass (Ninth Episode — wicked stepmother/sleeping draught)

Calderón — Play adapted by Voltaire (Belarius/mountaineer parallel)

Forman, Dr. Simon — The Book of Plays and Notes thereof (MS Ashm. 208), 1610–11

Ayrer, Jakobus — Comedia (1618) on wager-plot

Solbergk, Zachariah Lubhold von — The Beautiful Story of a God-fearing Merchant of Padua (German play, 1596)

V. Other Persons Referenced

Furness, Horace Howard — Editor of this New Variorum Edition (died before completion)

Furness, H. H., Jr. — Completed publication; wrote Foreword, October 1913

Charles, Dr. Benson B. — University of Pennsylvania; compiled the Index

Bunnett, Miss F. E. — Translator of Gervinus's Commentaries

Schmitz, Miss L. Dora — Translator of Ulrici

Black, John — Translator of Schlegel's Lectures

Payne, John — Translator of Boccaccio (Villon Society)

Laroche, Benjamin — French translator

Le Tourneur — French translator

Hugo, François-Victor — French translator

Rusconi, Carlo — Italian translator

Carcano, Giulio — Italian translator

Collins, William — Poet; 'A Song Sung by Guiderius and Arviragus over Fidele'

Spohr, Louis — Composer; opera Euryanthe based on La Violette

Tennyson, Alfred, Lord — On Pericles (partial Shakespeare authorship)

Beaumont, Francis and Fletcher, John — Philaster; The Faithful Shepherdess

Rose, Edward — New Sh. Soc. Transactions essay on sudden emotion

Ingram, Prof. John K. — Light and weak endings tests

Stokes, H. P. — On Date of Composition

Lee, Sir Sidney — D.N.B. article on Dr. Simon Forman

Tout, Prof. T. F. — D.N.B. article on Cunobelinus

Voltaire — Adaptation of Calderón

Grosart, A. B. — Reprinted Davies's Works

Swinburne, A. C. — A Study of Shakespeare

Davies, John of Hereford — Scourge of Folly (entered Stat. Reg. 1610)

Stephen, Leslie — Called Warburton's dogmatism 'grotesque audacities'

Who are the New Variorum Critics?

Critics Cited in the New Variorum Edition of Macbeth

Edited by Horace Howard Furness (1873)

This document provides a comprehensive list of all critics, commentators, editors, scholars, translators, actors, and other persons cited in the New Variorum Edition of Shakespeare's Macbeth. Where the volume's "List of Books" (pp. 547–555) identifies the specific work from which annotations are drawn, that bibliographic information is included.

I. Editors Collated in the Textual Notes

These are the editions systematically collated in the textual apparatus, as listed on pp. 545–546 of the volume.

Editor Abbreviation Edition(s) / Date(s)
Rowe, Nicholas Rowe i, Rowe ii 1st ed. 1709; 2nd ed. 1714
Pope, Alexander Pope i, Pope ii 1st ed. 1723; 2nd ed. 1728
Theobald, Lewis Theob. i, Theob. ii 1st ed. 1733; 2nd ed. 1740
Hanmer, Sir Thomas Han. 1744
Warburton, William Warb. 1747
Capell, Edward Cap. c. 1761

Johnson and Steevens Var. '73, Var. '78, Var. '85 1773; 1778; 1785

Steevens, George Steev. 1793

Singer, S. W. Sing. i, Sing. ii 1st ed. 1826; 2nd ed. 1856
Knight, Charles Knt i, Knt ii 1st ed. c. 1841; 2nd ed. 1865
Collier, J. Payne Coll. i, Coll. ii, Coll. iii 1st ed. 1842; 2nd ed. 1858; 3rd ed. 1877
Dyce, Alexander Dyce i, Dyce ii, Dyce iii 1st ed. 1857; 2nd ed. 1866; 3rd ed. 1875
Staunton, Howard Sta. 1860
White, R. Grant Wh. i, Wh. ii 1st ed. 1861; 2nd ed. 1883

Keightley, Thomas Ktly 1865
Wright, W. Aldis Cla. Clarendon Press Series, 1869
Hudson, H. N. Huds. ii, Huds. iii 2nd ed. 1871; School Shakespeare 1879
Rolfe, W. J. Rlfe 1877
Deighton, K. Dtn 1889
Additional Editions Consulted (Not Fully Collated)

These editions were consulted in disputed passages and their editors' views recorded, but not collated word for word.

Harness, W. — 1830

Clark, W. G. and Wright, W. A. — Globe Edition, 1864

Delius, Nicolaus — Elberfeld, 1869

Hunter, Rev. John — Longman's Series, 1870

Moberly, C. E. — Rugby Edition, London, 1872

Marshall, F. A. — Henry Irving Edition, 1888

Chambers, E. K. — Arden Shakespeare, London, 1896

Manly, J. M. — Longman's English Classics, Boston, 1896

Thurber, S. — Academy Series, Boston, 1896

Pattee, F. L. — Silver Series, 1897

Sherman, L. A. — New York, 1899

McWilliam, R. — Swan Edition, London, 1899

Sprague, H. B. — 1900

Verity, A. W. — Pitt Press Shakespeare, London, 1901

Gollancz, Israel — Temple Shakespeare, n.d.

II. Complete List of Books and Authors Cited

The following is the complete "List of Books" from pp. 547–555 of the volume, listing all works from which annotations, commentary, and critical excerpts are drawn. An asterisk (*) marks books used in the preparation of the revised (1903) edition.

III. Actors and Theatre Figures Discussed

The revised edition includes an account of actors from Garrick to the early 1900s. The following performers and theatre figures are discussed in the volume.

Bernhardt, Sarah (Madame) — Actress

Betterton, Thomas — Actor, associated with D'Avenant's version (1670s)

Booth, Edwin — American tragedian; prompt-book edited by W. Winter (1878)

Calcraft, Mr. — Theatre manager

Darling, Miss — Actress

Forbes-Robertson, J. — Actor

Garrick, David — Actor; extensively discussed in Actors appendix

Irving, Sir Henry — Actor-manager, Lyceum Theatre; contributed critical essays

Kean, Charles — Actor/producer

Kemble, John Philip — Actor; author of Macbeth Reconsidered (1786)

Kemble, Mrs. F. A. (Fanny) — Actress; wrote on characters in Macbeth (1867)

Lacy — Actor (painted in Charles II's time)

Macklin, Charles — Actor; innovator in costume

Macready, William Charles — Actor; Reminiscences cited

Martin, Lady (Helena Faucit) — Actress; Some of Shakespeare's Female Characters (1891)

Melvin, Mr. — Actor, Covent Garden (1806–7)

Phelps, Samuel — Actor/producer

Pritchard, Mrs. Hannah — Actress

Sheridan, Richard Brinsley — Theatre manager

Siddons, Mrs. Sarah — Actress; her Lady Macbeth extensively discussed

IV. Historical Sources, Chroniclers, and Dramatists

Bellenden, John — Translator of Boece's chronicles

Boece (Boethius), Hector — Scottish historian; source material for Holinshed

Buchanan, George — Scottish historian

Burton, Robert — Anatomy of Melancholy

D'Avenant, Sir William — Adapted Macbeth (1673 and 1674 versions reprinted in appendix)

Fordun, John of — Scottish chronicler

Guthrie, William — Scottish historian

Gwynne, Dr. Mathew — Author of Latin interlude performed at Oxford before James I

Heylin, Peter — Cosmography (source for D'Avenant's 'Argument')

Holinshed, Raphael — Chronicles (1587) — primary source; passages reprinted in appendix

Middleton, Thomas — The Witch (scenes reprinted in appendix); Works (ed. Dyce, 1843)

Nixon, Anthony — The Oxford Triumph (1605)

Scot, Reginald — Discoverie of Witchcraft (1584)

Scott, Sir Walter — Miscellaneous Essays; historical discussions

Skene, W. F. — Scottish historian (costume)

Tighernach — Irish chronicler

Topsell, E. — History of Serpents (London, 1608)

Weyer, Johann — Demonologist

Wintoun, Andrew of — Scottish chronicler

V. Other Persons Referenced

Additional literary figures, musicians, artists, and others referenced in the volume but not primarily as Shakespearean critics.

Addison, Joseph — Dramatist (Cato)

Byron, Lord — Poet; influence discussed

Chappell — Music historian (Music appendix)

Defoe, Daniel — Referenced for costume

Dryden, John — Poet/dramatist

Fitzgerald, Edward — Translator

Ford, John — Dramatist

Grimm, Jacob — Folklorist

Heywood, Thomas — Dramatist

Kyd, Thomas — Dramatist; Spanish Tragedy parallels discussed

Locke, Matthew — Composer of music for D'Avenant's Macbeth

Lowell, James Russell — American critic

Massinger, Philip — Dramatist

Milton, John — Poet (contemplated Macbeth as subject)

Nashe, Thomas — Terrors of the Night

Retzsch, Moritz — Artist (Outlines)

Southey, Robert — Poet

Spenser, Edmund — Poet

Swinburne, A. C. — Literary critic

Thomas, L. F. — Proof-reader acknowledged in preface

Waldron, F. G. — Dramatic editor; identified Hunter as author of Harry Rowe's Macbeth

Wister, Mrs. A. L. — Translated German passages for the edition

Where the volume's "List of Books" identifies the specific work from which annotations are drawn, that bibliographic information is included.

Abbott, E. A.: Shakespearian Grammar — London, 1870

Badham, C.: Criticism applied to Shakespeare — 1846

Badham, C.: The Text — 1856

Bailey, S.: The Received Text — 1862–66

Bathurst, C.: Shakespeare's Versification — 1857

Becket, Andrew: Shakespeare's Himself Again — 1815

Bucknill, J. C.: Medical Knowledge of Shakespeare — 1860

Bucknill, J. C.: The Mad Folk of Shakespeare — 1867

Bulloch, J.: Studies on the Text — 1878

Capell, Edward: Notes and Various Readings — 1759, 1779

Cartwright, R.: New Readings — 1866

Deverell, Robert: Discoveries in Hieroglyphics, etc. — 1813

Ludwig, Otto: Shakespeare-Studien — Leipzig, 1872

Benedix, R.: Die Shakespearomanie — Stuttgart, 1873

Friesen, Freiherr von: Will. Shakspere's Dramen, etc. — Wien, 1876

Koppel, Dr. Richard: Textkritische Studien über Richard III und King Lear — Dresden, 1877

Schmidt, Dr. Alexander: King Lear (Deutsche Shakespeare-Gesellschaft) — Berlin, 1879

III. Actors and Theatre Figures

Burbage, Richard — Original Lear (c. 1605); elegy quoted

Betterton, Thomas — Acted Lear 1662–1672 in Tate's version

Garrick, David — Great 18th-century Lear; used Tate's version

Quin — Admired as Lear before Garrick

Barry, Spranger — Rival Lear to Garrick; fine voice

Cooke, George Frederick — Played Lear

Kemble, John Philip — Acted Lear in Tate's version (from 1783)

Kean, Edmund — Greatest early 19th-century Lear; restored Shakespeare's fifth act (1823)

Booth, Junius Brutus — Played Lear at Covent Garden (1820)

Macready, William Charles — Restored Shakespeare's original text at Covent Garden (1838)

Booth, Edwin — Prompt-Book published 1878

Devrient, Louis — Distinguished German Lear; discussed by Ulrici

Rossi — Acted King Lear (Academy review, 1876)

Possart, Ernst — German stage Lear (München, 1875)

Kean, Charles — Edition/production (1858)

Tate, Nahum — Adapted King Lear (1681); held stage for 160 years

IV. Historical Sources, Chroniclers, and Dramatists

Geoffrey of Monmouth — Historia Britonum — earliest chronicle source for Lear story

Layamon — Brut — early retelling of Lear legend

Holinshed, Raphael — Chronicle — direct/indirect source; passages reprinted in Appendix

Spenser, Edmund — Faery Queene — retelling of Lear story

Sidney, Sir Philip — Arcadia — source of Gloucester subplot

King Leir (anonymous, 1605) — Ante-Shakespearian drama; probable direct source

Harsnett, Dr. Samuel — Declaration of Popish Impostures (1603) — source for Edgar's language

Camden, William — Remains — contains the Lear story

Gesta Romanorum — Contains a version of the story

Mirour for Magistrates — Contains the story

V. Other Persons Referenced

Furness, Horace Howard — Editor of this New Variorum Edition

Furness, Rev. Dr. (father) — Translations of German Criticisms

Norris, J. Parker — Prepared the English Bibliography

Lamb, Charles — Essay arguing Lear cannot be acted

Addison, Joseph — The Spectator, No. 40 (1711)

Dana, R. H. — The Idle Man (criticism of Kean's Lear)

Scott, Sir Walter — Quarterly Review essay on costume

Godwin, E. W. — Architecture and Costume of Shakespeare's Plays (The Architect)

Dryden, John — Preface to The Spanish Friar

Goethe — Cited by Kreyssig as having pronounced the first scene 'absurd'

Schmitz, L. Dora (Miss) — Translator of Ulrici

Black, John — Translator of Schlegel's Lectures

Swinburne, A. C. — Fortnightly Review essay on Cordelia

Hugo, Victor — William Shakespeare (French, quoted in Tate's Version section)

Arber, Edward — Transcript of Stationers' Registers

Weiss, J.: Wit, Humor, etc., Hamlet — Boston, 1876

Werder, Karl: Vorlesungen über Hamlet (at Berlin University, 1859–72) — Berlin, 1875

Whalley, P.: Enquiry into the Learning of Shakespeare

White, R. Grant: Shakespeare's Scholar — New York, 1854

Whiter, W.: Specimen of a Commentary — London, 1794

Wordsworth, Bishop Charles: Shakespeare and the Bible

German Translations

Wieland: Shakespeare's theatralische Werke, vol. viii — Zurich, 1766

Eschenburg: Shakespeare's Schauspiele, vol. xii — Zurich, 1782

Schlegel, A. W.: Shakespeare's dramatische Werke, vol. iii — Berlin, 1798

Heufeld: Die Bearbeitung von Heufeld — Pressburg, 1773

Schroeder, F. L.: Hamlet, Ein Trauerspiel in sechs Aufzügen — Hamburg, 1777/1778

Benda, J. W. O.: Shakespeare's dramatische Werke, vol. xiii — Leipzig, 1826

Voss, J. H.: Shakespeare's Schauspiele, vol. viii — Stuttgart, 1827

Schlegel und Tieck: Shakespeare's dramatische Werke, vol. vi — Berlin, 1832

Ortlepp, Ernst: Shakespeare's dramatische Werke, vol. i — Stuttgart, 1838

Keller, A. und Rapp, M.: Shakespeare's Schauspiele, vol. vi — Stuttgart, 1846

Sievers, E. W.: Shakespeare's Dramen für weitere Kreise — Leipzig, 1851–52

Jencken, Dr. F.: Shakespeare's Dramen (2nd ed. 1856) — Mainz, 1853–55

Bodenstedt, Friedrich: Shakespeare's dramatische Werke, vol. xxv — Leipzig, 1870

Moltke, Max: Shakespeare's sämmtliche Werke, vol. vi — Leipzig, n.d.

Oechelhauser, W.: Shakespeare's dramatische Werke für die deutsche Bühne — Berlin, 1870

Devrient, Eduard und Otto: Deutscher Bühnen und Familien Shakespeare, vol. i — Leipzig, 1873

Elze, Karl (ed.): Deutsche Shakespeare-Gesellschaft edition — Berlin, 1869

III. Actors and Theatre Figures

Betterton, Thomas — Earliest major Hamlet; described by Cibber

Garrick, David — Hamlet alteration (1771); described by Lichtenberg

Henderson — Hamlet at the Haymarket (1777)

Kemble, John Philip — Altered Hamlet for Drury Lane (1796)

Kean, Edmund — Major 19th-century Hamlet

Macready, William Charles — Major 19th-century Hamlet

Hackett, James H. — American actor; Notes, Criticisms, etc. (1863)

Forrest, Edwin — American actor

Booth, Edwin — American actor; major Hamlet interpreter

Fechter, Charles — Controversial interpretation

Irving, Henry — Recent London success noted in Preface

Salvini, Tommaso — Acting edition (1873)

Rossi — Italian actor

Brockmann — First great German Hamlet

Dawison — German actor

Devrient, Emil — German actor

Talma — French actor; performed Ducis's Hamlet

Schroeder, F. L. — German actor-manager; Hamlet version

IV. Historical Sources, Chroniclers, and Dramatists

Saxo Grammaticus — Danish historian; earliest source of the Hamlet story (Historia Danica)

Belleforest, François de — Histoires tragiques; adapted Saxo's story

The Hystorie of Hamblet — English prose translation from Belleforest (London, 1608)

Der Bestrafte Brudermord / Fratricide Punished — Old German tragedy; translated in Vol. II

Nashe, Thomas — Epistle prefixed to Greene's Menaphon (1589); earliest allusion to a Hamlet play

Kyd, Thomas — Probable author of the lost ur-Hamlet

Ducis, J. F. — Hamlet, Tragédie imitée de l'Anglais en vers Français (1769)

Voltaire — Critique of Shakespeare; Théâtre complet (1768)

V. Other Persons Referenced

Furness, Horace Howard — Editor of this New Variorum Edition

Furness, Rev. Dr. (father) — Translated all German extracts in Vol. II

Furness, H. H., Jr. — Copyright holder (1918)

Wister, Mrs. A. L. (sister) — Translated Freiligrath's 'Deutschland ist Hamlet'

Furness, Mrs. (wife) — Prepared Index in Vol. I and Table of Contents in Vol. II

Allen, Professor — Former collaborator, deceased

Fish, A. I. — Prepared the English Bibliography

Norris, J. Parker — Selections re Actors' Interpretations

Thomas, L. F. — Reader of proofs

Collier, J. Payne — Editor/scholar; acknowledged for assistance

Cohn, Albert — German scholar; assistance acknowledged

Corson, Professor — Scholar; Jottings on the Text

Crosby, Joseph — Acknowledged for assistance

Fleay, Rev. F. G. — Scholar; acknowledged

Elze, Prof. Dr. Karl — German scholar; acknowledged

Furnivall, F. J. — Scholar; acknowledged

Hudson, Rev. H. N. — Editor/scholar; acknowledged

Rolfe, W. J. — Scholar; acknowledged

Rushton, W. L. — Scholar; acknowledged

Timmins, S. — Reprints of Quartos; acknowledged

White, R. Grant — Editor; acknowledged

Wright, W. Aldis — Cambridge editor; acknowledged

Ingleby, Dr. C. M. — Scholar; special acknowledgment for suggestions

Goethe — Wilhelm Meisters Lehrjahre (1795); central to German Hamlet criticism

Lessing — Hamburgische Dramaturgie (1767)

Schlegel, A. W. — Über dramatische Kunst und Litteratur (1811)

Horn, Franz — Shakespeare's Schauspiele erläutert (1825)

Heine, Heinrich — Shakespeare's Mädchen und Frauen (1839)

Werder, Karl — Vorlesungen über Hamlet at Berlin University (1859–72; pub. 1875)

Freiligrath, Ferdinand — Poet; 'Deutschland ist Hamlet'

Schmitz, L. Dora (Miss) — Translator of Ulrici

Black, John — Translator of Schlegel's Lectures

Lichtenberg, G. Chr. — Critic of Garrick's acting (Briefe aus England)

Lamb, Charles — English critic

Swinburne, A. C. — Fortnightly Review essay

Lowell, James Russell — American critic

Ducis: Roméo et Juliette (adaptation) — Paris, 1772

Chateaubriand: Shakspere ou Shakspeare — 1801

Girardin, Saint-Marc: Cours de Littérature Dramatique — Paris, 1845/1855

Villemain: Etudes de Littérature Ancienne et Etrangère — Paris, 1849

Chasles, Philarète: Etudes sur Shakespeare — Paris, 1851

Lamartine, A. de: Shakspeare et son Oeuvre — Paris, 1865

Taine, Hippolyte: Littérature Anglaise — Paris, 1866

Hugo, Victor: William Shakespeare — Paris, 1864

III. Actors and Theatre Figures

Garrick, David — Acting Copy; version of the death-scene discussed

Booth, Edwin — Acting Copy; staging notes throughout

Otway, Thomas — Author of Caius Marius (1712), an adaptation

Ducis — French theatrical adaptation (1772)

Howard, James — Altered Romeo and Juliet into a tragi-comedy (c. 1660)

Davenant, Sir William — Company performed Howard's version

Pepys, Samuel — Saw performance March 1, 1661–2

Zingarelli, Nicolo — Composer of Romeo and Juliet opera

Bellini, Vincenzo — Composer of I Capuleti e i Montecchi

Berlioz, Hector — Sinfonie Dramatique (1835)

IV. Historical Sources, Chroniclers, and Dramatists

Da Porto, Luigi — Original Italian novella (d. 1529; first printed Venice, 1535), La Giulietta

Bandello, Matteo — Italian novel on same subject, published Lucca, 1554

Boisteau, Pierre — French version of story, from Bandello

Belleforest, François de — Histoires Tragiques (continuing Boisteau's translations)

Brooke, Arthur — English poem, The Tragicall Historye of Romeus and Juliet (1562)

Painter, William — Palace of Pleasure, vol. ii (1567), 'Rhomeo and Julietta'

Masuccio di Salerno — Novellino (1476), earliest version of story elements

Groto, Luigi — Hadriana, Italian drama (1578)

Della Corte, Girolamo — History of Verona, relates story as fact occurring 1303

Lope de Vega — Los Castelvines y Monteses (Spanish play)

Meres, Francis — Palladis Tamia (1598); attributed play to Shakespeare

Danter, John — Publisher of Q1 (1597)

Creede, Thomas — Printer of Q2 (1599)

V. Other Persons Referenced

Furness, Horace Howard — Editor of this New Variorum Edition

Furness, H. H., Jr. — Copyright holder (1913)

Furness, Rev. Dr. (father) — Translated German passages in Appendix

Wister, Mrs. A. L. (sister) — Translated French passages in Appendix

Allen, Professor George — University of Pennsylvania; conjectures and theory on pronunciation

Fish, A. I. — Dean of The Shakspere Society of Philadelphia; lent library

Forrest, Edwin — Lent Second and Third Folios

Bunnett, F. E. (Miss) — Translator of Gervinus's Commentaries

Schmitz, L. Dora (Miss) — Translator of Ulrici

Black, John — Translator of Schlegel's Lectures

Lewes, George Henry — Life of Goethe; criticized Goethe's version

Madame de Staël — Cited by Hallam on the passion of love

Voltaire — Cited by Lessing and Lamartine as critic of Shakespeare

Schiller — Cited by Tieck; preface to The Bride of Messina

Elze, Karl — Replied to Rümelin in Jahrbuch for 1867

Bernays, Michael — Commented on Schlegel's translation

Lloyd, W. W. — Critic in Singer (ed. 2); extensive passages on source and date

Lennox, Mrs. — Critic; said Shakespeare invented only death of Paris and created Mercutio

Walker, Joseph Cooper — Historical Memoir on Italian Tragedy (London, 1799)

Swinburne, A. C. — Referenced as critic

Marston, John — Tenth Satire references Romeo and Juliet at the Curtain Theatre

Chalmers, George — Assigns composition to spring of 1592

Burbage, Richard — First actor of Othello; Elegy at his death 1618

Field, Nathan — May have succeeded Burbage as Othello

Burt — Acted the Moor (Pepys, 1660)

Mohun — Acted Iago (Pepys, 1668)

Clun — Previously acted Iago

Hart — Acted Cassio (Pepys, 1668)

Hughs, Mrs. — Performed Desdemona at Drury Lane, 1663

Saunderson, Mrs. (later Mrs Betterton) — Possibly the first English actress; played Desdemona

Garrick, David — Acted Othello in Venetian dress

Quin — Remarked on Garrick's Othello costume

Barry — Acted Othello in gold-laced scarlet suit

Kemble, J. P. — Acted Othello at Drury Lane, 1785

Siddons, Mrs. — Acted Desdemona opposite Kemble

Kean, Edmund — The great Othello of the modern stage; last appearance 25 March 1833

Kean, Charles — Acted Iago opposite his father Edmund Kean

Kemble, Charles — Present at Kean's last performance

Tree, Ellen — Acted Desdemona at Kean's last performance

Booth, Edwin — Prepared Prompt-Book notes for Furness; acted Othello and Iago

Fechter, Charles — Acting edition; half-caste interpretation of Othello

Salvini — Used Giulio Carcano's Italian translation

Rossi — Used Giulio Carcano's Italian translation

Forrest, Edwin — American actor

Jordan, Thomas — Wrote the Prologue introducing a female actress

Knolles, Richard — History of the Turks; source for historical context of Cyprus/Rhodes

Brown, Rawdon — Identified 'Christofal Moro' as possible historical Othello

Sanuto, Marin — Venetian diarist whose records Brown used

Klein, J. L. — Found parallels with Ludovico Dolce's Marianna

Dolce, Ludovico — Author of Marianna (1565)

Simrock, Karl — Quellen des Shakespeare; discussed source and origin

Tylney, Edmund — Master of the Revels, 1579–1610

Buck, Sir George — Master of the Revels; licensed Othello registration

Walkley, Thomas — Stationer who published the First Quarto, 1622

Hawkins, Richard — Published Second Quarto, 1630

Cunningham, Peter — Discovered/forged Revels Books entries

Lessing — Referenced by Ludwig

What's New Variorum?

What's New Variorum?

The New Variorum Shakespeare is one of the most comprehensive scholarly editions of Shakespeare's works, representing over a century of meticulous textual scholarship. First begun in 1871 by Horace Howard Furness, this monumental project aimed to collect and synthesize centuries of Shakespeare commentary, textual variants, and critical interpretations into definitive scholarly editions.

Furness devoted the rest of his life to this project, personally completing 18 volumes between 1871 and his death in 1912. His first volume, Romeo and Juliet, appeared in 1871, followed by Macbeth (1873), Hamlet (two volumes, 1877), King Lear (1880), and others through The Tempest (1892) and Twelfth Night (1901). His son, Horace Howard Furness Jr., continued the work, and after 1936, the Modern Language Association took over the project, continuing to publish new volumes into the 21st century—though the complete works remain unfinished, testament to the sheer scope of the undertaking.

Each New Variorum volume is essentially a massive scholarly apparatus that surrounds Shakespeare's text. For any given line or word, it presents textual variants from different early editions, editorial commentary spanning from the 18th century through modern scholarship, glosses of difficult passages, critical interpretations, performance history, and source materials Shakespeare drew upon.

Revolutionary Digital Access

What we've accomplished here is groundbreaking: we've taken these massive, dense scholarly volumes—traditionally accessible only in research libraries and often intimidating even to scholars—and made them instantly accessible at the click of a button. Through advanced RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) technology, we've digitized and indexed the entire New Variorum commentary corpus, creating something that has never existed before: instant, contextual access to centuries of Shakespeare scholarship.

When you click on any line or word, you're not just getting a random note—our system intelligently retrieves the exact relevant commentary from the New Variorum editions. This means that obscure phrase that's puzzled you? There's probably a 19th-century scholar who spent years thinking about it. That textual crux you're wondering about? You can instantly see how editors from 1733 to today have tried to solve it.

This is the first time anyone has been able to experience Shakespeare with this level of scholarly depth at their fingertips. We've transformed what used to require hours of library research into an immediate, interactive experience. You're not just reading Shakespeare—you're joining a 300-year conversation about every single word, with the greatest Shakespeare scholars in history as your guides, all powered by cutting-edge AI technology that brings their insights directly to you.

Used under fair use for educational purposes. Sources include JSTOR, academic publications, and historical editions. Full citations provided with each annotation.

Who are the New Variorum Critics?

Critics Cited in the New Variorum Edition of Henry IV, Part 1

Edited by Samuel Burdett Hemingway, Ph.D. (Professor of English, Yale University)

This document provides a comprehensive list of all critics, commentators, editors, scholars, translators, actors, and other persons cited in the New Variorum Edition of Shakespeare's Henry IV, Part 1. Where the volume's "List of Books" identifies the specific work from which annotations are drawn, that bibliographic information is included.

Pope, Thomas — Possible original Falstaff (conjectured by Baldwin)

Lowin, John — Known Falstaff before the wars (attested by James Wright)

Burbage, Richard — Original Prince Hal (conjectured by Baldwin)

Betterton, Thomas — Hotspur, then Falstaff (1700 revival, Lincoln's Inn Fields)

Quin, James — Falstaff (Lincoln's Inn Fields/Covent Garden, 1720s–1753)

Garrick, David — Hotspur (Covent Garden, 1746; only five performances)

Henderson, John — Falstaff (Bath, 1772; Haymarket, 1777; Drury Lane, Covent Garden)

Shuter, Ned — Falstaff (Covent Garden, 1753–1776)

Kemble, John Philip — Hotspur (Drury Lane, 1791–1817, last appearance)

Cooke, George Frederick — Falstaff (Covent Garden, 1802ff; also American tours)

Stephen Kemble — Falstaff 'without stuffing' (Drury Lane, 1802ff)

Kean, Edmund — Hotspur (Drury Lane, 1819–1820)

Macready, William Charles — Hotspur (Covent Garden, 1818; Drury Lane, 1830–1834)

Dowton, William — Falstaff (Drury Lane, 1812–1837)

Hackett, James H. — Falstaff (American, 1828–1870s; London, 1839, 1845)

Phelps, Samuel — Falstaff (Sadler's Wells, 1846–1860; Drury Lane, 1864, 1869)

Kean, Charles — Hotspur (Princess's Theatre, 1850)

Hallam, Lewis — Hotspur (Chapel Street Theatre, New York, 1761)

Cooper, Thomas Abthorpe — Hotspur then Falstaff (American career)

IV. Historical Sources, Chroniclers, and Dramatists

Holinshed, Raphael — Chronicles of England (2nd ed., 1587) — primary source for plot

Hall, Edward — Union of the Two Noble Famelies of Lancastre and Yorke (1550) — historical source

Daniel, Samuel — The Ciuile Wars (1595) — poetic rendering of events, used by Shakespeare

Hayward, Sir John — Life of King Henry IV (1599) — possible source

The Famous Victories of Henry the Fifth — Anonymous (c. 1594; Quarto 1598) — comic source

Phaer, Thomas — How Owen Glendour (in Mirrour for Magistrates, 1559)

Stow, John — Annales of England (1592)

Grafton, Richard — Chronicles of England (1569)

Bale, John — A Brefe Chronycle Concernynge Sir Johan Oldecastell (1548)

James, Dr. Richard — Legend and Defence of Sir John Oldcastle (1600)

Meres, Francis — Palladis Tamia, Wit's Treasury (1598)

V. Other Persons Referenced

Hemingway, Samuel Burdett — Editor of this volume; Professor of English, Yale University

Adams, Joseph Quincy — General Editor of NV series

Tucker Brooke, C. F. — Chairman of Supervisory Committee

Shaaber, Matthias A. — Member of Supervisory Committee

Schelling, Felix E. — Member of Supervisory Committee

Spencer, Hazelton — Member of Supervisory Committee

Gibson, Martha Jane — Compiled the Index

Furness, Horace Howard — Founder of the New Variorum series (1833–1912)

Furness, Horace Howard, Jr. — Continued the series (1865–1930)

Morgann, Maurice — Essay on the Dramatic Character of Sir John Falstaff (1777) — landmark essay

Bradley, A. C. — The Rejection of Falstaff (1902) — major critical essay

Stoll, Elmer E. — Falstaff (1914/1927) — counter-argument to Morgann/Bradley

Priestley, J. B. — The English Comic Characters (1925) — on Falstaff

Tolman, Albert H. — Why Did Shakespeare Create Falstaff? (1919)

Knight, G. Wilson — The Imperial Theme (1931)

Hotson, J. Leslie — Shakespeare versus Shallow (1931)

Cowl, R. P. — Sources of the Text of Henry IV (1927–1929)

Pollard, A. W. — Shakespeare Folios and Quartos; Shakespeare's Fight with the Pirates

Wilson, John Dover — The Elizabethan Shakespeare; The Essential Shakespeare

Chambers, Sir Edmund K. — The Elizabethan Stage; William Shakespeare

Schmitz, L. Dora — Translator of Ulrici

Bunnett, F. E. — Translator of Gervinus

Black, John — Translator of Schlegel

Shaw, G. B. — Critic of Tree's Falstaff (1896)

Swinburne, A. C. — A Study of Shakespeare

Pater, Walter — Shakespeare's English Kings (in Appreciations, 1889)

Wise, Andrew — Publisher of Q1 (1598)

Short, P. [Peter] — Printer of Q1 (1598)

Dering, Sir Edward — Reviser of the Henry IV manuscript (c. 1624)

Where the volume's "List of Books" identifies the specific work from which annotations are drawn, that bibliographic information is included.

III. Actors and Theatre Figures

Terry, Ellen — Played Puck as a child (~10 years old) in Charles Kean's revival

Kean, Charles — Revived the play; described by Fontane

Phelps, Samuel — Revived the play at Sadler's Wells

Calvert, Charles — Revived the play at Manchester

Benson, F. R. — Revived the play at the Globe Theatre, London

Daly, Augustin — Revived the play at his Theatre, January 31, 1888

Mendelssohn, Felix — Composed the music for Tieck's Berlin production at Potsdam

Purcell, Henry — Composed music for The Fairy Queen opera (1692)

Tieck, Ludwig — Directed the celebrated Berlin/Potsdam production under Friedrich Wilhelm IV

Kempe, William — Actor in Shakespeare's company

Davenant, Sir William — Mentioned for The Siege of Rhodes; granting women's parts on stage

Reynolds — Altered the play for Covent Garden revival

IV. Historical Sources, Chroniclers, and Dramatists

Plutarch (via North's translation) — Source for Theseus, Hippolyta, Perigenia, Ariadne

Chaucer — Knight's Tale as possible source for Theseus as Duke of Athens

Ovid (Metamorphoses) — Source for name Titania; Golding's and Sandys's translations

Spenser — Faerie Queene for fairy world and Oberon's lineage

Huon of Bordeaux — French romance; source for the character of Oberon

Wolfram von Eschenbach — Ballad of 'Otnit'; original of dwarf Elberich/Oberon

Gryphius, Andreas — Absurda Comica, Or Herr Peter Squentz (1663), adapted from Shakespeare

Kirkman — The Wits (1672/3); contains The Humours of Bottom the Weaver

Henslowe, Philip — Diary recording performance of 'hewen of burdokes' (1593)

Meres, Francis — Wits Commonwealth (1598); earliest mention of the play

Fisher, Thomas — Stationer; published the First Quarto (1600)

Roberts, James — Printer; published the Second Quarto (1600)

V. Other Persons Referenced

Furness, Horace Howard — Editor of this New Variorum Edition

Furness, H. H., Jr. — Copyright holder, 1923

Lamb, Charles — Noted that Shakespeare 'invented the fairies'

Lee, S. L. — Introduction to Duke Huon of Burdeaux (E.E.T.S.)

Poe, E. A. — Quoted: 'When asked for a definition of poetry, I think of Titania and Oberon'

Lessing — Quoted on 'feasts of the gods'

De Vinne, T. L. — The Invention of Printing (New York, 1876)

Grimm, Brothers — Mythologie; cited by Simrock for Midsummer customs

Schwenter, Daniel — Introduced Peter Squentz play at Altdorf, Germany

Paracelsus — Mentioned by Gervinus for Theory of Elemental Spirits

Humboldt, Alexander von — Present at Tieck's Berlin performance

Friedrich Wilhelm IV — King of Prussia; patron of Tieck's production

Goethe — Faust (Walpurgisnachtstraum Intermezzo discussed)

Schmitz, L. Dora — Translator of Elze's and Ulrici's works into English

Black, John — Translator of Schlegel's Lectures

Swinburne, A. C. — Fortnightly Review essay (1876)

Jonson, Ben — Masques compared unfavorably with Shakespeare

Milton — Comus; fairy parallels

Spencer, John — Puritanical preacher; admonitory letter re 1631 performance

Williams, Bishop John — Bishop of Lincoln; hosted 1631 private performance

Musical Settings and Composers

Arne, Dr. Thomas Augustine: Settings of 'Under the greenwood tree' and 'Blow, blow, thou winter wind'

Linley, William: Supplemented Arne's settings; vocal arrangements

Bishop, Sir Henry: Harmonised Arne's melodies; operatised AYLI settings

Smith, John Stafford: Glee settings of songs

Danby, John: Glee composer; arrangement of Arne's melody

Carey, Henry: The Huntsman's Song in Love in a Forest (1723)

Stevens, R. J.: Settings of songs as glees

Wesley, Samuel (the younger): Setting of 'Blow, blow' (c. 1830)

Somers, Hon. Mrs. Dyce: Setting of 'Blow, blow'

Zimmerman, Agnes: Setting of 'Blow, blow' (reviewed 1863)

Schachner, R.: Part-song setting (1865)

Bartholomew, Mrs. A. S. (first Mounsey): Part-song (1857)

Macfarren, G. A.: Part-songs (1864, 1869)

Warsing, H. N.: Part-song (1878)

Thiele, Ed. (Hofkapellmeister in Dessau): Settings for Oechelhauser's German adaptation

Sullivan, Sir Arthur S.: 'Rosalind' solo (1865)

Parry, C. H. Hubert: 'Spring Song' (1874)

Chilcot, Thomas: Setting of 'Wedding is great Juno's crown' (c. 1740)

Tours, B.: Part-song (1882)

III. Actors and Theatre Figures

Siddons, Mrs. — Played Rosalind; discussed by Boaden, Campbell

Jordan, Mrs. — Rosalind; rival to Mrs Siddons; praised by Campbell, Mrs Inchbald

Kemble, J. P. — Played Jaques; praised by Mrs Inchbald; Acting Copy 1815

Garrick, David — Cited for acting traditions

Martin, Lady (Miss Helen Faucit) — Rosalind; extensive criticism from Shakespeare's Female Characters (1884)

Macready, William Charles — Suppressed the cuckoo song in Drury-Lane revival

Johnson, Charles — Adapted AYLI as Love in a Forest (1723, Drury Lane)

Cibber, Colley — Theatre manager who produced Love in a Forest

Sand, George — Adapted AYLI as Comme il vous plaira for the French stage

IV. Historical Sources, Chroniclers, and Dramatists

Lodge, Thomas — Author of Rosalynde, or Euphues' Golden Legacy (1590), the primary source

The Tale of Gamelyn — Possible indirect source (c. mid-14th century); reprinted from Skeat's edition

Meres, Francis — Palladis Tamia (1598); AYLI not mentioned, used as evidence for date

Roberts, James — Stationer; Furness's theory about the 'staying' of AYLI

Arber, Edward — Editor of Transcript of the Stationers' Registers

V. Other Persons Referenced

Furness, Horace Howard — Editor of this New Variorum Edition

Furness, Rev. Dr. (father) — Translated extracts from Kreyssig for the editor

Furness, H. H., Jr. — Copyright holder, 1918

Dowden, Edward — Criticism of Jaques; classification of plays into periods

Furnivall, F. J. — Classification of plays into periods; Leopold Shakspere (1877)

Delius, Nicolaus — Comparison of Lodge and AYLI in Shakespeare Jahrbuch, vol. vi

Zupitza — Analysis of Tale of Gamelyn in Shakespeare Jahrbuch, vol. xxi

Stone, W. G. — Essay in New Shakspere Society's Transactions (1882)

Ingram — Verse-tests; New Shakspere Society's Transactions (1874)

Spedding, James — Advocate of metrical tests for chronology

Gifford, William — Editor of Ben Jonson; quoted on Every Man Out of his Humour

Jonson, Ben — Every Man Out of his Humour; Tieck's theory of rivalry with Shakespeare

Kempe, William — Actor; Fleay's theory about 'clowns' vs 'fools'

Godwin, E. W. — The Architect (1875); extensive essay on costume for AYLI

Gautier, Théophile — Quoted on national humor

North, Christopher (John Wilson) — Quoted on theatrical illusion and time

Inchbald, Mrs. — Criticism of AYLI (1808)

Schmitz, L. Dora (Miss) — Translator of Ulrici's Shakespeare's Dramatic Art

Black, John — Translator of Schlegel's Lectures

Seward, Anna — Wrote on Mrs Siddons as Rosalind

Regnier — George Sand's letter addressed to him

Monson, Lady — Translator of George Sand's preface

Appian: History of Rome (trans. H. White) — 1899

Ayres, H. M.: Shakespeare's Julius Caesar in the Light of Some Other Versions

Badham, C.: Text of Shakespeare — Cambridge, 1856

III. Actors and Theatre Figures

Betterton, Thomas — Acted Caesar; earliest recorded revival c. 1671

Booth, Barton — Brutus and Caesar at Theatre Royal, 1706

Booth, Edwin — Cassius, then Brutus (1864, 1871, 1876); Antony

Booth, J. B. — Cassius (Chatham Garden, 1828); described by Gould

Booth, J. W. — Antony at Winter Garden, 1864 (with E. Booth and J. B. Booth Jr.)

Cibber, Colley — Described Betterton's Brutus

Cooke, George Frederick — Actor

Forrest, Edwin — Antony (Bowery Theatre, 1827)

Garrick, David — Mentioned in acting traditions

Irving, Sir Henry — Production designed by Alma Tadema (never carried out for Julius Caesar)

Kean, Charles — Brutus (Bowery Theatre, 1839)

Kemble, Charles — Antony at Covent Garden, 1812

Kemble, John Philip — Brutus at Covent Garden/Drury Lane, 1812–1817

Macready, William Charles — Cassius (first time 1818); Brutus

Mansfield, Richard — Brutus (Grand Opera House, Chicago, 1902); production described by Winter

Mantell, R. B. — Brutus in Shakespearean repertoire

Phelps, Samuel — Brutus; farewell performance 1862

Quin, James — Brutus

Sheridan, R. B. — Brutus at Covent Garden, 1774

Tree, Herbert Beerbohm — Antony at Her Majesty's Theatre, 1898/1900; described by Simpson

Faversham, William — Antony (Buffalo, 1912); production described by Bennett

Young, Charles Mayne — Cassius; 'no person living had seen so good a Brutus'

Wilks — Antony at Drury Lane

IV. Historical Sources, Chroniclers, and Dramatists

Plutarch — Lives of Julius Caesar, Brutus, and Mark Antony — primary source via North's translation

North, Sir Thomas — Translator of Plutarch's Lives (editions 1579, 1595, 1603, 1612)

Amyot, Jacques — French translator of Plutarch; North translated from his version

Appian — Civil Wars; speech of Antony possibly used by Shakespeare

Suetonius — Lives of the Caesars (trans. Holland, 1606); doubtful use by Shakespeare

Dion Cassius — Annals of the Roman People; no translation until 1700

Alexander, Sir William (Earl of Stirling) — Julius Cæsar (tragedy, 1607; reprinted in Appendix)

Grévin, Jacques — César (French tragedy, 1561)

Muret, Marc Antoine — Julius Cæsar (Latin tragedy, c. 1553)

Chapman, George — Cæsar and Pompey (tragedy, published 1631)

Anon. — The Tragedy of Cæsar and Pompey, or Cæsar's Revenge (Trinity College, Cambridge, 1605/1607)

Borck, Caspar Wilhelm von — First German translation of Shakespeare (Julius Caesar, 1741)

Voltaire — La Mort de César; translation of parts of Shakespeare's Julius Caesar

Eedes, Richard — Epilogus Cæsaris Interfecti (Latin play, Oxford, 1582)

Gosson, Stephen — The History of Cæsar and Pompey (mentioned in Playes Confuted, 1581)

Meres, Francis — Palladis Tamia (1598); does not enumerate Julius Caesar

Weever, John — Mirror of Martyrs (1601); reference to speeches of Brutus and Antony

Holinshed, Raphael — Chronicles

V. Other Persons Referenced

Furness, Horace Howard, Jr. — Editor of this New Variorum Edition

Furness, Horace Howard (Sr.) — Founder of the series; 'He to whom I owe the deepest obligations'

Jastrow, Dr. Morris, Jr. — Librarian of the University of Pennsylvania

Charles, Dr. Benson B. — University of Pennsylvania; compiled the Index (in Cymbeline volume)

Abbot, George M. — Philadelphia Library

Bunnett, Miss F. E. — Translator of Gervinus's Commentaries

Schmitz, L. Dora — Translator of Ulrici's Dramatic Art

Black, John — Translator of Schlegel's Lectures

Common, Thomas — Translator of Nietzsche's Joyful Wisdom

Cary, Miss E. J. — Translator of Stapfer's Shakespeare and Classical Antiquity

Jonson, Ben — Discoveries; Staple of News; Every Man out of his Humour; Catiline

Drayton, Michael — Baron's Wars (comparison passage)

Drummond of Hawthornden — Recorded Jonson's conversations

Digges, Leonard — Commendatory verses in First Folio mentioning Julius Caesar

Dryden, John — Preface to Troilus & Cressida (comparison of quarrel scenes)

Swinburne, A. C. — Critic

Dowden, Edward — Critic; Shakespeare: His Mind and Art

Brandes, Georg — William Shakespeare

Moulton, R. G. — Shakespeare as Dramatic Artist

Gervinus, G. G. — Shakespeare Commentaries

MacCallum, M. W. — Shakespeare's Roman Plays

Stapfer, Paul — Shakespeare and Classical Antiquity

Allen, J. C. — Poet Lore article on Caesar's character

Paton, A. P. — Notes on North's Plutarch in Greenock Library

Alma Tadema, Sir Lawrence — Designed scenery for Tree's production

Collier, J. Payne — Editor and scholar

Dante Alighieri — Inferno (Brutus and Cassius in lowest circle)

Boissier, G. — Portrait bust of Brutus described

Michelangelo — Bust of Brutus at the Uffizi

III. Actors and Theatre Figures

Garrick, David — Played King John and the Bastard at Drury Lane, 1745 and later

Delane — Played King John at Covent Garden, 1737; also Bastard at Drury Lane

Quin, James — Played King John at Covent Garden, 1750

Barry, Spranger — Played Faulconbridge at Covent Garden, 1750

Cibber, Mrs. Susanna — Played Constance at Drury Lane, 1745, and Covent Garden, 1750

Woffington, Mrs. — Played Constance at Covent Garden, 1751

Sheridan, Thomas — Played King John at Drury Lane, 1760

Yates, Mrs. — Played Constance at Drury Lane, 1760

Kemble, John Philip — Played King John at Drury Lane, 1783 and later; retired 1817

Siddons, Mrs. Sarah — Played Constance at Drury Lane, 1783–1812; famous in the role

Kemble, Charles — Played Faulconbridge at Covent Garden

Kemble, Frances Anne (Fanny) — Played Constance at Covent Garden, 1831

Kean, Edmund — Played King John at Drury Lane, 1818

Macready, William Charles — Played King John at Covent Garden, 1823, Drury Lane, 1842

Phelps, Samuel — Played King John at Sadler's Wells, 1844; also Faulconbridge

Kean, Charles — Played King John at Princess's Theatre, 1852, and in America

Kean, Mrs. Charles — Played Constance opposite Charles Kean

Glyn, Miss — Played Constance at Manchester, 1847; later at Sadler's Wells

Tree, Herbert Beerbohm — Played King John at Crystal Palace, 1889; Her Majesty's Theatre, 1899

Benson, F. R. — Played King John in England

Waller, Lewis — Played Faulconbridge at Her Majesty's Theatre, 1899

Neilson, Julia — Played Constance at Her Majesty's Theatre, 1899

Mantell, Robert B. — Played King John in America, 1907–1908; major interpretation

Russell, Marie Booth — Played Constance opposite Mantell

Neumann, Christiane — Played Arthur at Weimar Court Theatre, 1791; coached by Goethe

Booth, J. B. (the elder) — Played King John at Bowery Theatre, 1834

Booth, Edwin — Noted for death scenes; never acted King John

Irving, Henry — Noted for death scenes

Modjeska, Madame — Played Constance in American provinces

O'Neill, Miss — Played Constance at Covent Garden, 1816

Faucit, Mrs. — Played Constance at Covent Garden, 1812

IV. Historical Sources, Chroniclers, and Dramatists

Holinshed, Raphael — Chronicler; principal historical source

Hall, Edward — Chronicler; cited by Steevens as closely followed

Stowe, John — Chronicler

Grafton, Richard — Chronicler; Chronicle, or History of England

Matthew Paris — Chronicler; Chronica Majora

Roger of Wendover — Chronicler; Flowers of History

Walter of Coventry — Chronicler; Historical Collections (ed. Stubbs)

Meres, Francis — Author of Palladis Tamia (1598); listed King John

Bale, John, Bishop of Ossory — Author of Kynge Johan, earliest dramatic treatment

Henslowe, Philip — Theatre owner; Diary cited for dating evidence

Peele, George — Playwright; author of The Battle of Alcazar

Wilson, Robert — Playwright; co-author of Funeral of Richard Cordelion

Chettle, Henry — Playwright; co-author of Funeral of Richard Cordelion

Munday, Anthony — Playwright; co-author of Funeral of Richard Cordelion

Drayton, Michael — Playwright; co-author of Funeral of Richard Cordelion

Kyd, Thomas — Playwright; author of The Spanish Tragedy

Norgate, Kate — Historian; John Lackland (London, 1902)

V. Other Persons Referenced

Furness, Horace Howard, Jr. — Editor of this volume

Furness, Horace Howard, Sr. — Editor of earlier volumes; copyright holder

Abbot, George M. — Librarian of the Philadelphia Library

Folger, H. C. — Collector; placed Folio collections at editor's disposal

Morgan, J. Pierpont — Collector; placed Folio collections at editor's disposal

Leo, F. A. — Scholar; prepared list of Cruces for all plays

Cibber, Colley — Author of Papal Tyranny in the Reign of King John (1745)

Valpy, R. — Head-Master of Reading School; produced alteration of King John (1800)

Tieck, Ludwig — German critic; maintained Shakespeare wrote The Troublesome Raigne

Schlegel, A. W. — German critic and translator

Coleridge, S. T. — Critic; attempted chronological classification of plays

Gentleman, Francis — Actor and critic; Dramatic Censor

Reed, Isaac — Editor of Biographia Dramatica and Steevens' editions

Boswell, James (the younger) — Editor of Malone's posthumous Variorum (1821)

Tyrwhitt, Thomas — Scholar; emendation adopted in Cambridge text

Ingram, J. K. — Professor; verse tests for chronology

Pulling, F. S. — Scholar; speech-ending test

Clarke, F. W. — Scholar; editor of Old Spelling Shakespeare

Gollancz, Israel — Editor of Temple Shakespeare; dated King John 1595

Hunt, Leigh — Editor of The Tatler (1831); reviewed F. A. Kemble's Constance

Planche, J. R. — Costume historian; Cyclopedia of Costume

Lacroix, Paul — Costume historian; Manners, Customs and Dress During the Middle Ages

Goethe, Johann Wolfgang von — Coached Christiane Neumann as Arthur; author of elegy Euphrosyne

Macaulay, Thomas Babington — Historian; designated King John 'a trifler and a coward'

Churchill, Charles — Poet and satirist; cited for verse criticisms of actors

Bunnett, Miss — Translator of Gervinus's Commentaries

Schmitz, L. D. — Translator of Ulrici's Dramatic Art

Black, John — Translator of Schlegel's Lectures

Schmidt, Alexander — Lexicographer; Shakespeare-Lexicon

Swinburne, A. C. — Critic; A Study of Shakespeare

Davenant, Sir William — Cited for perversion of Macbeth

Tate, Nahum — Cited for desecration of King Lear

Dryden, John — Cited for travesty of The Tempest

Winter, William — Critic; Shakespeare on the Stage (Third Series, 1916)

Who are the New Variorum Critics?

Critics Cited in the New Variorum Edition of Love's Labour's Lost

Edited by Horace Howard Furness, M.A. (Harv.)

This document provides a comprehensive list of all critics, commentators, editors, scholars, translators, actors, and other persons cited in the New Variorum Edition of Shakespeare's Love's Labour's Lost. Where the volume's "List of Books" identifies the specific work from which annotations are drawn, that bibliographic information is included.

I. Editors Collated in the Textual Notes

II. Complete List of Books and Authors Cited

The following lists all works from which annotations, commentary, and critical excerpts are drawn. An asterisk (*) marks books added for a revised edition where applicable.

Abbott, E. A.: Shakespearian Grammar — London, 1870

Arbeau, Thoinot: Orchesographie (Reprint by Laure Fonta, Paris, 1888) — Lengres, 1588

Arrowsmith, W. R.: Shakespeare's Editors and Commentators — London, 1865
