III. Actors and Theatre Figures

Burbage, Richard — Actor; mentioned in Walter Cope's letter (1604) recommending the play for performance

Kempe, William — Actor of clowns; identified by Fleay as possibly represented by Costard

Garrick, David — Actor; cited for principle of losing 'no drop of that immortal man'

Daly, Augustin — Arranged Love's Labor's Lost in Four Acts for stage (New York, 1891)

Goldingham, Harry — Actor at Kenilworth entertainment (1575)

IV. Historical Sources, Chroniclers, and Dramatists

Monstrelet — Chronicler; his Chronicles provide the historical foundation of the plot

Johnes, Thomas — Translator of Monstrelet's Chronicles, 1810

Favin, Andre — Author of Histoire de Navarre, Paris, 1602

Davila — Author of Memoirs of Civil Wars in France

Sismondi — Historian; cited for Catherine de Medici's 'l'escadron volant'

Caro, Dr. J. — Historian; found parallels between Shakespeare's plots and Russian history (Eng. Studien, 1878)

Bartholomjess, Christian — Author of Giordano Bruno; suggested Albrecht Laski as model for Armado

Cope, Walter — Author of 1604 letter to Lord Cranborne recommending revival of the play

Pissemsky, Theodore Andreievitch — Russian ambassador to London c.1582; his visit possibly influenced the Muscovites scene

Fletcher, Giles — Elizabeth's envoy to Russia; his Description of the Russe Commonwealth (1591) cited

Harington, Sir John — Author of The Metamorphosis of Ajax, 1596

Laski, Albrecht — Polish visitor to Elizabeth's court; suggested as possible model for Armado

Meres, Francis — Author of Wit's Treasury (1598), which mentions the play

Tofte, Robert — Author of Alba (1598), wherein the play is mentioned by name

V. Other Persons Referenced

Furness, Horace Howard — Editor of this New Variorum Edition

Clark, W. G. — Co-editor of Cambridge Edition (First Edition, 1863)

Wright, W. Aldis — Co-editor of Cambridge Edition; sole editor of Second Edition (1891)

Reed, Isaac — Editor of Reed's Steevens Variorum editions (1803, 1813)

Boswell, James (the younger) — Editor of Boswell's Malone Variorum (1821)

Farmer, Dr. Richard — Believed Warburton right about Florio/Holofernes identification

Dryden, John — Poet/dramatist; his views on rhyme and early plays cited

Gifford, William — Editor of Ben Jonson's works

Lyly, John — Dramatist; author of Euphues; his influence on Shakespeare discussed extensively

Greene, Robert — Dramatist; imitator of Lyly's style; accused Shakespeare of being 'Johannes fac-totum'

Lodge, Thomas — Writer; imitator of Lyly's Euphuistic style

Harvey, Gabriel — First to call Lyly's style 'Euphuisme'

Nashe, Thomas — Pamphleteer; identified by Fleay as possibly represented by Moth

Munday, Antony — Stage-plotter; identified by Fleay as possibly represented by Antony Dull

Cooper, Bishop — Identified by Fleay as possibly represented by Holofernes

Peele, George — Dramatist; plays analyzed by Fleay for Anti-Martinist allusions

Jonson, Ben — Dramatist; Fastidious Brisk discussed in relation to Armado

Sidney, Sir Philip — Author of Defence of Poesie and Arcadia; his Rombus compared to Holofernes

Walsingham, Sir Francis — Mentioned as friend of Thomas Wilson

Grosart, Dr. Alexander B. — Editor of Southwell's Poems; detected parallels with Berowne's speech

Mulcaster, Richard — Schoolmaster; conjectured by some as original of Holofernes

Hunt, Thomas — Shakespeare's schoolmaster at Stratford (1572–1577); suggested by Elze as original of Holofernes

Jenkins, Thomas — Shakespeare's schoolmaster; suggested by Elze as original of Sir Hugh Evans

Churchill, George B. — Author (with Keller) of account of Latin dramas at English universities

Keller, Wolfgang — Author (with Churchill) of account of Latin dramas

Beyersdorff — Proved that Holofernes cannot be traced to Giordano Bruno's Manfurio

Landmann, Dr. F. — Author of essay on Lyly's influence on Shakespeare (New Shakespeare Society)

Goodlet, John — Author of essay on Lyly (Eng. Studien, V, 1882)

Child, Clarence Griffin — Author of essay on John Lyly and Euphuism

Guevara — Spanish author; credited by Landmann as inventor of Euphuistic style

North, Sir Thomas — Translator; introduced Guevara's style into England (1557)

Pettie, George — Author of A petite Pallace; exhibited Euphuistic elements before Lyly

Góngora, Don Luis de — Spanish author of estilo culto style

Sylvester, Joshua — Translator of Dubartas's Divine Weekes

Dubartas — French poet; his style discussed as modified Euphuism

Ronsard — French poet; representative of affected style in France

Cornwallis, Sir William (the younger) — His Essays (1600) contain description of Florio

Daniel (the poet) — Noted as Florio's brother-in-law

Campbell, Thomas — Critic; his commentary on the play cited

Swinburne, A. C. — Critic; his essay on the play cited

Méziéres, A. — French critic; Shakespeare, ses Oeuvres et ses Critiques, 1865

Roderick — Author of Remarks; cited as source of verse-test methodology

Stokes, H. P. — Critic; fixed date of play as 1591–2

Sarrazin, Dr. G. — German critic; essays in Shakespeare Jahrbuch (xxix–xxxii, 1894–1896)

Schmidt, Alexander — Author of Shakespeare-Lexicon

Schmitz, L. Dora — Translator of Elze and Ulrici into English

Black, John — Translator of Schlegel's Dramatic Art into English

Vecellio, Cesare — Author of costume book (ed. 1598) cited by Knight

Who are the New Variorum Critics?

Critics Cited in the New Variorum Edition of Much Ado About Nothing

Edited by Horace Howard Furness, M.A. (Harv.)

This document provides a comprehensive list of all critics, commentators, editors, scholars, translators, actors, and other persons cited in the New Variorum Edition of Shakespeare's Much Ado About Nothing. Where the volume's "List of Books" identifies the specific work from which annotations are drawn, that bibliographic information is included.

I. Editors Collated in the Textual Notes

Editor Abbreviation Date(s)

Hudson, H. N. [Huds.] 1880
Rolfe, W. J. [Rlfe.] 1880

Deighton, K. [Dtn.] 1888

III. Actors and Theatre Figures

Garrick, David — Actor of Benedick; praised by Francis Gentleman

Pritchard, Mrs. — Actress of Beatrice; performed opposite Garrick

Woffington, Mrs. — Actress of Beatrice; received considerable applause

Jordan, Mrs. — Actress of Beatrice; noted for her hearty laugh

Faucit, Helen (Lady Martin) — Actress of Beatrice from 1846; extensively reviewed

Macready, William Charles — Actor of Benedick

Irving, Henry — Actor of Benedick from 1882 at the Lyceum

Terry, Ellen — Actress of Beatrice from 1882 at the Lyceum opposite Irving

Kemble, Charles — Actor of Benedick; Lady Martin played Beatrice to his farewell

Kemble, Fanny — Mentioned as Charles Kemble's daughter, actress

Kempe, William — Actor of Dogberry in the original production; name appears in Quarto

Cowley — Actor of Verges in the original production; name appears in Quarto

Wilson, Jack — Named in Folio stage directions as musician/singer (Balthazar)

Bryan, George — English actor who travelled in Germany

Pope, Thomas — English actor who travelled in Germany

Drousiano — Italian Commediante in London, 1577–8

IV. Historical Sources, Chroniclers, and Dramatists

Ariosto — Orlando Furioso; story of Ariodante and Genevra as remote analogue

Harington, Sir John — Translator of Ariosto's Orlando Furioso (1591/1634)

Turbervil, George — Wrote English verse version of the Ariodante-Genevra story (not extant)

Beverley, Peter — Author of history of Ariodanto and Jeneura (entered 1565)

Bandello, Matteo — Italian novelist; principal source of the Hero-Claudio plot

Belle-Forest, F. de — French adapter of Bandello's novel in Histoires Tragiques

Ayrer, Jacob — German dramatist; Die schoene Phoenicia, based on Belle-Forest (1618)

Heinrich Julius, Duke of Wolfenbüttel — German dramatist; Comedia von Vincentio Ladislao (1599)

Chariton — Ancient Greek novelist; Chaereas and Callirrhoe as ultimate source (Urquelle)

Martorell, Johan — Author of Tirante el Blanco (c. 1400)

Cinthio, Giraldi — Author of Hecatomithi; Ninth story cited as parallel

Starter — Dutch dramatist; tragi-comedy based on Bandello (1618)

Timoneda, Juan — Spanish author of El Patrañuelo (1576)

Mulcaster, Richard — His children performed 'A Historie of Ariodante and Geneuora' before the Queen, 1582

Meres, Francis — Author of Palladis Tamia (1598); central to dating question

V. Other Persons Referenced

Furness, Horace Howard — Editor of this New Variorum Edition

Wister, Mrs. Annis Lee — Furness's sister; translated German criticism extracts

Wright, W. Aldis — Cambridge editor

Malone, Edmond — Editor; dated the play early 1600

Chalmers, George — Dated the play to autumn 1599

Brae, A. E. — Argued Much Ado is the lost Love's Labour's Won

Fleay, F. G. — Critic; argued date of 1597–98; identified 'Deformed' as allusion to Shakespeare

Furnivall, F. J. — Critic; detected political allusion to Essex

Simpson, Richard — Critic; disputed Furnivall's interpretation

Ingram, Professor — Compiled table of Light and Weak Endings for dating

Dowden, Edward — Critic; placed Much Ado in 'Later Comedy' group

Digges, Leonard — Contemporary of Shakespeare; praised popularity of Beatrice and Benedick

Roberts, James — Stationer; possible enterer of 'to be staied' plays

Wise, Andrew — Stationer; entered Much Ado, 23 August 1600

Aspley, William — Stationer; co-enterer with Wise

Simmes, Valentine — Printer of the Quarto

Field, Richard — Printer of Venus and Adonis and Lucrece

Arber, Edward — Editor of Transcript of the Stationers' Registers

Heywood, Thomas — Fellow-actor and dramatist; quoted on stolen texts

Sidney, Sir Philip — Wrote letter mentioning 'Will' (Kempe) as Leicester's player

Jonson, Ben — Dramatist; Cynthia's Revels cited for 'Amorphus' parallel

Gifford, William — Editor of Jonson

De Vinne, T. L. — Author of The Invention of Printing (1876)

Cunningham, Peter — Editor of Extracts from the Accounts of the Revels

Sarrazin, G. — Identified characters with historical persons in Shakespeare Jahrbuch (1899)

Cartwright, Robert — Identified Don Pedro = Raleigh; Claudio = Southampton

Hunter, Joseph — Identified Benedick with William Lord Herbert

Craik, George L. — Urged Taming of the Shrew as Love's Labour's Won

Spedding, James — Essay on Division of Acts

Clapp, Henry A. — Computed Duration of Action (Atlantic Monthly, March 1885)

Lang, Andrew — Critic; essay on Much Ado in Harper's Magazine, Sept. 1891

Wedmore, Frederick — Critic; reviewed Irving's Benedick in The Academy

Russell, Sir Edward R. — Reviewed Helen Faucit's Beatrice in Liverpool Daily Post

Davis, L. Clarke — Reviewed Irving and Terry in Philadelphia Inquirer

Oxberry, W. — Provided costume descriptions for Much Ado, 1823

Godwin, Edward W. — Architect and stage designer; wrote on staging in The Architect

Vecellio, Cesare — Brother of Titian; Habiti Antiche e Moderni cited on costume

Gosse, Edmund W. — Described Starter's Dutch tragi-comedy in The Athenaeum, 1877

Grimm, Herman — German scholar; found prototype of Benedick in Duke Heinrich Julius

Cohn, Albert — Author of Shakespeare in Germany (1865)

Tieck, Ludwig — German Romantic; translator of Shakespeare

Scott, Dr. Mary Augusta — Suggested Castiglione's Courtyer as influence on Beatrice

Charles I — Added 'Benedick and Beatrice' as second title in his copy of the Second Folio

Schmitz, L. Dora — Translator of Ulrici's Shakespeare's Dramatic Art

Black, John — Translator of Schlegel's Lectures

Swinburne, A. C. — Critic; A Study of Shakespeare (1880)

Schmidt, Alexander — Author of Shakespeare-Lexicon

Who are the New Variorum Critics?

Critics Cited in the New Variorum Edition of Richard the Third

Edited by Horace Howard Furness, Jr.

This document provides a comprehensive list of all critics, commentators, editors, scholars, translators, actors, and other persons cited in the New Variorum Edition of Shakespeare's Richard the Third. Where the volume's "List of Books" identifies the specific work from which annotations are drawn, that bibliographic information is included.

I. Editors Collated in the Textual Notes

III. Actors and Theatre Figures

Burbage, Richard — Original Richard III; mentioned in Funeral Elegy; wore red-haired wig

Betterton, Thomas — Listed by Downes as one of his best characters

Sandford, Samuel — Cited by Cibber as peculiarly qualified for Richard

Cibber, Colley — Adapted Richard III (first produced 9 July 1700); played Richard himself

Cibber, Theophilus — Played Richmond (1734)

Wilks — Played King Henry (1715)

Porter, Mrs. — Played Queen Elizabeth (1715)

Ryan — Played Richard at Lincoln's Inn Fields (1721)

Quin, James — Played Buckingham (1721); later played Richard (1734, 1742)

Garrick, David — First appearance as Richard, Goodman's Fields, 19 Oct. 1741; major role through 1776

Woffington, Mrs. — Played Lady Anne (1742)

Cibber, Mrs. — Played Lady Anne (1742)

Barry, Spranger — Played Richard at Covent Garden (1757); unsuccessful

Smith — Played Richard at Covent Garden (1761, 1774)

Siddons, Mrs. — Played Lady Anne (1776); played Queen Elizabeth (1813)

Sheridan, R. B. — Played Richard at Covent Garden (1776)

Henderson, John — Played Richard at Drury Lane (1777)

Kemble, J. P. — Played Richard at Drury Lane (1789); revised Cibber's alteration

Cooke, George Frederick — Played Richard at Covent Garden (1800); critiqued by Lamb and Hunt

Kemble, Charles — Played Richmond (1811); played Richard (1813)

Kean, Edmund — First London Richard at Drury Lane, 13 Feb. 1814; critiqued by Hazlitt

Booth, J. B. (Junius Brutus) — Played Richmond (1819); later performed as Richard

Macready, William — First played Richard at Covent Garden, 25 Oct. 1819; restored Shakespeare's text 1831

Phelps, Samuel — Produced condensed Shakespeare version at Sadler's Wells, 1845

Forrest, Edwin — American actor; Richard III described at length by Alger

Booth, Edwin — Restored Shakespeare's Richard III at Booth's Theatre, NY, 7 Jan. 1878

Irving, Henry — Produced from Shakespeare's text at Lyceum, 29 Jan. 1877

Mansfield, Richard — Produced new arrangement, Philadelphia, 4 Nov. 1889

Pope (actor) — Played Henry VI

IV. Historical Sources, Chroniclers, and Dramatists

More, Sir Thomas — Author of Biography of Richard III, basis of Hall's and Holinshed's chronicles

Hall, Edward — Author of Chronicles of England, principal chronicle source

Holinshed, Raphael — Author of Chronicles, source for the play (2nd ed. 1586–7)

Vergil, Polydore — Author of Historia Angliae

Rous, John — Author of Historia Regum Angliae; contemporary chronicler

Ingulph — Chronicle of Croyland Abbey

Grafton, Richard — Continued More's history; included it in his Chronicles

Cardinal Morton — Attributed as possible actual author of More's Life of Richard III

Fabian — Contemporary chronicler of Richard

Legge, Thomas — Author of Latin play Richardus Tertius, acted at Cambridge c. 1579

Seagar(s), Francis — Author of Legend of King Richard III in Mirrour for Magistrates (1559)

Niccols, Richard — Author of new legend of Richard III in Mirrour for Magistrates (1610)

Weever, John — Author of Epigrammes (1599), mentioning Richard as Shakespeare's character

Meres, Francis — Author of Palladis Tamia (1598), enumerating Richard III

Walpole, Horace — Author of Historic Doubts about Richard III (1768)

Buck, Sir George — Author of Life of Richard the Third (1646)

Stow, John — Author of Survey of London

V. Other Persons Referenced

Furness, Horace Howard, Jr. — Editor of this New Variorum edition

Furness, Horace Howard (Sr.) — The editor's father and counsellor

Rollins, Hyder Edward — Named on title page

Shaaber, Matthew Adam — Named on title page

Dana, Charles E. — Acknowledged for references to Heraldry and Armour

Jastrow, Dr. Morris, Jr. — Librarian of the University of Pennsylvania

Sims, Valentine — Printer of Q1 (1597)

Wise, Andrew — Publisher of Q1–Q3

Creede, Thomas — Printer of Q2–Q5

Law(e), Mathew — Bookseller for Q4, Q5, Q6

Purfoot, Thomas — Printer of Q6 (1622)

Norton, John — Printer of Q7 (1629) and Q8 (1634)

Spedding, James — Critic; major paper on Quarto vs. Folio text

Pickersgill, E. H. — Critic; paper on Quarto and Folio of Richard III

Daniel, P. A. — Critic; Introduction to Griggs' Facsimile; Time Analysis

Wright, W. Aldis — Cambridge editor

Clark, W. G. — Cambridge editor (First Edition)

Marshall, F. A. — Editor (1888); analysis of text and date

Schmidt, Alexander — German scholar; textual analysis

Koppel, R. — German scholar; Textkritische Studien on Richard III

Ingram, Professor — Contributed paper on versification tests

Bunnett, Miss F. E. — Translator of Gervinus's Commentaries

Schmitz, L. Dora — Translator of Ulrici's Dramatic Art

Black, John — Translator of Schlegel's Lectures

Scott, Sir Walter — Critic of Kemble's Richard

Lamb, Charles — Critic; wrote on G. F. Cooke's Richard

Hazlitt, William — Critic; wrote on Kean's Richard

Robinson, H. Crabb — Diarist; wrote on Kean's Richard

Russell, Sir Edward — Critic; wrote on Irving's Richard for The Theatre

Peele, George — Playwright; Fleay attributed original play partly to Peele

Marlowe, Christopher — Playwright; influence on Richard III extensively discussed

Jonson, Ben — Playwright; wrote a play on Richard III c. 1603

Manningham, John — Diarist; anecdote about Shakespeare and Burbage (1601)

Corbet, Bishop — Poet; Iter Boreale, reference to Burbage as Richard

Nashe, Thomas — Author; references to Latin tragedy in Have With You to Saffron Walden (1596)

Harington, Sir John — Author; Metamorphosis of Ajax (1596)

Fox, Charles — Statesman; compared Clarence's dream to death of Alcestis

Hogarth — Painter; left picture of Garrick in the tent scene

Swinburne, A. C. — Critic; Study of Shakespeare

Voltaire — Referenced by Kreyssig and Lounsbury as critic of Shakespeare

Schiller — Quoted by Kreyssig on tragic art

Who are the New Variorum Critics?

Critics Cited in the New Variorum Edition of The Merchant of Venice

Edited by Horace Howard Furness, Ph.D., LL.D., L.H.D.

This document provides a comprehensive list of all critics, commentators, editors, scholars, translators, actors, and other persons cited in the New Variorum Edition of Shakespeare's The Merchant of Venice. Where the volume's "List of Books" identifies the specific work from which annotations are drawn, that bibliographic information is included.

I. Editors Collated in the Textual Notes

Editor Abbreviation Date(s)
Roberts (First Quarto) [Q1] 1600
Heyes (Second Quarto, Ashbee's Facsimile) [Q2] 1600
First Folio [F1] 1623
Second Folio [F2] 1632

Third Folio [F3] 1664

Rowe, Nicholas (Second Edition) [Rowe ii] 1714

Pope, Alexander (Second Edition) [Pope ii] 1728

Theobald, Lewis (Second Edition) [Theob. ii] 1740

Warburton, William [Warb.] 1747
Johnson, Dr. Samuel [Johns.] 1765
Capell, Edward [Cap.] c. 1766

Johnson and Steevens [Steev. '78] 1778

Clark, W. G. and Wright, W. A. (Globe) [Glo.] 1864
Keightley, Thomas [Ktly] 1864
Cowden-Clarke, Charles and Mary [Clarke] c. 1864
Dyce, Alexander (Second Edition) [Dyce ii] 1866
Clark and Wright (Clarendon) [Cla.] 1869
Delius, Nicolaus (Third Edition) [Del.] 1872
Dyce, Alexander (Third Edition) [Dyce iii] 1875
Collier, J. Payne (Third Edition) [Coll. iii] 1877
Hudson, H. N. [Huds.] 1879
Rolfe, W. J. [Rlfe] 1883
White, R. Grant (Second Edition) [Wh. ii] 1883

Musical Settings and Composers

Horn, Charles: Composed duet from 'All that glisters is not gold'

Stevenson, Sir John: Well-known setting of 'Tell me where is fancy bred'

Arne, Dr. Thomas: Earliest setting of 'Tell me where is fancy bred' (solo for Mrs Clive)

Stevens, R. J. S.: Setting for three sopranos and tenor

Linley, William: Setting as dialogue duet with chorus

Hatton, John: Setting sung by Miss Poole with female chorus

Richmond, Rev. L.: Round setting, c. 1810–1820

Bartholomew, M. (Mrs Mounsey): Part Song setting (S., A., T., B.)

Macfarren, G. A.: Part Song setting — 1869

Luetzen, B.: Duettino setting — Brighton, 1877

Pinsuti, C.: Part Song settings — c. 1880

Calcott, J. G.: Part Song settings — 1883

Sullivan, Sir Arthur: Duet for soprano and tenor, in Cantata of Kenilworth — 1865

Blanchard, T.: Song setting

King, M. P.: Trio setting of 'How sweet the moonlight'

Dignum, Charles: Duet setting of 'How sweet the moonlight' — c. 1800

Hutchinson, Thomas: Duet setting of 'How sweet the moonlight' — c. 1807

Percy, John: Solo setting of 'How sweet the moonlight'

Leslie, Henry: Part-song setting of 'How sweet the moonlight' — 1866

Cooke, T.: Song setting of 'For do but note a wild and wanton herd'

Just, J. A.: German composer; turned Merchant of Venice into opera — Amsterdam, 1787

Petrella, Signor: Italian; turned Merchant of Venice into opera

Hassler, Simon: Set 'My bliss too long my bride denies' as Serenade-Barcarole — Philadelphia, 1878

III. Actors and Theatre Figures

Burbage, Richard — Original Shylock; mentioned in Funeral Elegy; wore red-haired wig

Dogget, Thomas — Comic actor; played Shylock in Lansdowne's Jew of Venice (1701)

Macklin, Charles — Revived Shakespeare's original 1741 at Drury Lane; transformed Shylock from comic to tragic

Kean, Edmund — First appeared as Shylock 26 Jan. 1814 at Drury Lane in black wig

Irving, Henry — Performed Shylock at Lyceum; dignified, sympathetic interpretation

Booth, Edwin — Provided prompt-book and letters on Shylock to Furness; traditional interpretation

Cooke, George Frederick — Actor of Shylock, compared to Macklin

Henderson, John — Actor of Shylock

Kemble, Stephen — Played Shylock; failed at Drury Lane before Kean

Huddart — Played Shylock from Dublin; failed at Drury Lane before Kean

Dowton — Played Shylock with innovation of Jewish friends in court

Reinecke — First serious Shylock on German stage c. 1780

Macready, William Charles — First to interpret Shylock as venerable Hebrew martyr/avenger

Kean, Charles — Acting Edition 1858; spoke Shylock free of dialect

Woffington, Mrs. (Peg) — Played Portia; praised by Gentleman

Clive, Mrs. (Kitty) — Played Portia; also sang Dr Arne's setting

Siddons, Mrs. — Played Portia; 'triumphant delirium of joy' in casket scene

Pope, Mrs. — Actress; played Portia; present at Macklin's last appearance

Bannister, Jack — Actor; gave Kean encouraging word on debut night

Oxberry, William — Actor; gave Kean brandy and water on debut night

Rae — Actor; played Bassanio to Kean's Shylock

Raymond — Acting manager at Drury Lane during Kean's debut

Arnold — Stage-manager at Drury Lane during Kean's debut

Ryder — Actor; understudied Shylock for Macklin's last appearance

Phelps, Mr. — Manager of Sadler's Wells

Glynn, Miss — Played Portia in Horne's Dramatic Reverie at Sadler's Wells

Drew, John — Played Tubal with Edwin Booth

Booth, Junius Brutus — Edwin's father; played Shylock in traditional manner

Jordan, Thomas — Actor and ballad-writer; wrote 'The Forfeiture' ballad (1664)

Tokely — Actor from Exeter; obtained first appearance at Drury Lane before Kean

Horne, Richard Hengist — Author and actor of 'Shylock, A Dramatic Reverie' (1850)

IV. Historical Sources, Chroniclers, and Dramatists

Robertes, James — Printer; entered Merchant of Venice in Stationers' Register 1598

Heyes, Thomas — Stationer; published Second Quarto 1600

Hayes, Laurence — Son of Thomas; re-entered play 1619; published Third Quarto 1637

Granville, George (Lord Lansdowne) — Author of The Jew of Venice (1701), adaptation

Higgons, Bevill — Wrote the Prologue to Lansdowne's Version

Lopez, Dr. Roderigo — Jewish physician executed 1594; possible prototype for Shylock

Manasseh Ben Israel — Jewish patriot; petitioned for re-admission of Jews to England (1655)

Discalzio, Ottonello — Professor of Law at Padua; prototype of Dr Bellario (identified by Elze)

Massuccio di Salerno — Italian novelist; source of abduction story

Ser Giovanni Fiorentino — Author of Il Pecorone; main source of plot

Boccaccio — Source for story elements

Gower, John — Confessio Amantis; source for casket story

Leti, Gregorio — Author of Life of Sixtus V

Coryat, Thomas — Author of Crudities; descriptions of Jews in Venice

Vecellio, Caesar — Described Venetian costumes

Meres, Francis — Palladis Tamia (1598); mentions the play

Goulart — Admirable Histoires (1620)

Silvayn, Alexander — The Orator (1596); analogue of bond story

V. Other Persons Referenced

Furness, Horace Howard — Editor of this New Variorum Edition

Furness, Rev. Dr. (father) — Translated selections from the German

Allen, Prof. George — Late professor; marginalia described as 'keen critic'

Corney, Bolton — Identified Fourth Quarto as Third Quarto with new title-page

Hales, Prof. J. W. — Detected interest of Fourth Quarto date (1652)

Smith, Miss Toulmin — Noted original of enlarged Burbage Elegy 'not yet come to light'

Edwards, Harry — Owner of Horne's original MS (New York)

Bulwer Lytton, Sir E. — Conducted journal where Horne's idea was first put forth

Cowen, Esek — Author of travestie 'Shylock v. Antonio' in Albany Law Journal (1872)

Lee, Sidney — Author of article on Dr Lopez and Jews in England

Inchbald, Mrs. — Believed Shakespeare's purpose was to 'hold up the Jew to detestation'

Bolingbroke, Lord — Invited Macklin to dinner after debut; gave him twenty guineas

Mansfield, Lord — Decided in Macklin's lawsuit on the right to hiss an actor

Pope, Alexander (poet) — Composed epitaph 'Here lies the Jew / That Shakespeare drew'

Godwin, E. W. — Architect; wrote on costume and scenery in The Architect (1875)

Gérome, Jean-Léon — Painter; Edwin Booth's costume inspired by his painting

Planché — Hist. of British Costume, cited on French hose

Waldron, F. G. — Late scholar; pencil notes on play cited by Hunter

Arber, Edward — Editor of Stationers' Register transcripts

Spedding, James — Critic; argued Shakespeare was man of business

Martin, Theodore — Critic; found no 'plea for toleration' in play

Marshall, Frank — Critic; Shakespeare was essentially a dramatist

Davis, Israel — Critic; compared Shakespeare to George Eliot

Anderson, David — Critic; no reason Jewish race should be ashamed of Shylock

Hawkins, Frederick — Critic; argued sympathy for Shylock was designedly aroused

Furnivall, F. J. — Scholar; contributed to The Theatre discussion on Shylock

Kemble, Mrs. (Fanny) — On Portia

Clarke, Helen A. (Miss) — Wrote on musical settings in Shakespeariana (Jan. 1888)

Schmitz, L. Dora (Miss) — Translator of Elze's Essays and Ulrici's Dramatic Art

Bunnett, F. E. (Miss) — Translator of Gervinus's Commentaries

Black, John — Translator of Schlegel's Lectures

Drury, Dr. — Head-master of Harrow; exclaimed 'He is safe!' at Kean's debut

Swinburne, A. C. — Critic; A Study of Shakespeare (1880)

Who are the New Variorum Critics?

Critics Cited in the New Variorum Edition of The Tempest

Edited by Horace Howard Furness

This document provides a comprehensive list of all critics, commentators, editors, scholars, translators, actors, and other persons cited in the New Variorum Edition of Shakespeare's The Tempest. Where the volume's "List of Books" identifies the specific work from which annotations are drawn, that bibliographic information is included.

III. Actors and Theatre Figures

Kemble, Miss (later Mrs Siddons) — Played Ariel at age 12, Worcester, 1767

Kemble, Mr. (Roger) — Manager, played Alonzo in 1767 performance

Siddons, Mr. — Played Hippolito in 1767 performance

Kemble, Mrs. F. A. (Fanny) — Actress and critic; wrote notes on the play (1860, 1882)

Martin, Lady (Helen Faucit) — Quoted on Miranda

Pepys, Samuel — Witnessed that the house was 'mighty full' for Dryden's version

IV. Historical Sources, Chroniclers, and Dramatists

Dryden and Davenant — Authors of the adapted Tempest; or, The Enchanted Island (1667)

Ayrer, Jacob — Author of Die schöne Sidea (The Fair Sidea), Nuremberg notary

Tieck, Ludwig — Discovered and promoted The Fair Sidea as a source

Calderón — Spanish playwright; Dryden's additions alleged to be borrowed from his play

Montaigne — Essays (esp. 'Of Cannibals'); translated by Florio, 1603; source for Gonzalo's speech

Strachey, William — Author of True Reportory of the Virginia wreck (c. 1612)

Jourdan, Silvester — Author of A Discovery of the Bermudas (pamphlet on Somers's shipwreck)

Somers, Sir George — His 1609 shipwreck on Bermuda was a major proposed source

Eden, Richard — Decades of the newe worlde (1555); source for 'Setebos'

Scot, Reginald — Discoverie of Witchcraft (1584); magical lore

Meres, Francis — Palladis Tamia (1598)

Ariosto — His tempest cited as parallel

Ovid — Medea's speech as source for Prospero's 'Ye elves'

V. Other Persons Referenced

Furness, Horace Howard — Editor of this New Variorum Edition

Furness, H. H., Jr. — Copyright holder, 1920

Furness, Rev. Dr. (father) — Translated extracts from Grimm, Horn, and Hugo

Allen, Prof. — Learned critic, Philadelphia Shakspere Society

Fish, A. I. ('The Dean') — Member of the Philadelphia Shakspere Society

Calver, Captain E. K., R.N. — Nautical expert consulted on seamanship

Spedding, James — Conjectural emendator cited in textual notes

Delius, Nicolaus — German Shakespeare scholar

Nicholson, Brinsley — Editor of Scot's Discoverie; commentator

Browning, Robert — Caliban upon Setebos; or Natural Theology in the Island

Goethe — Cited in comparison of Faust with Prospero

Darwin, Charles — Cited in Wilson's Caliban: the Missing Link

Lloyd, W. W. — Critical essay in Singer's 2nd ed. (1856)

Russell, Edward R. — Theological Review (1876); on Prospero

Strachey, Sir Edward — Quarterly Review (July 1890); on Ariel

Renan, Ernest — Caliban, philosophical drama (1878)

Heine, Heinrich — Shakespeare's Mädchen und Frauen (1839); on Miranda

Lessing — Germany's greatest Shakespearian scholar; quoted in Preface

Horn, Franz — Shakespeare's Schauspiele Erläutert (1823)

Montégut, Emile — French critic, Revue des Deux Mondes (1865)

Hugo, François-Victor — French translator and critic

Schlegel, A. W. — German critic; lectures translated by John Black

Grimm, Herman — German essayist; on Calderón and Dryden's version

Schmitz, L. Dora (Miss) — Translator of Ulrici's Shakespeare's Dramatic Art

Black, John — Translator of Schlegel's Lectures

Jonson, Ben — Bartholomew Fair allusion to The Tempest

Beaumont, Francis — Fleay suggested he may have abridged The Tempest for Court

Marlowe, Christopher — Identified allegorically with Caliban by Montégut

Rabelais — Parallelisms noted with The Tempest

Ingram, Professor — Metrical tables (New Sh. Soc. Trans.)

Smith, Miss T. R. — Prepared list of plays with Furnivall

Mulgrave, Lord — Provided nautical refutation of Dr Johnson's criticism

III. Actors and Theatre Figures

Garrick, David — Actor-manager, Drury Lane; produced Florizel and Perdita (1756)

Siddons, Mrs. — Played Hermione (first time 25 March 1802); famed for statue scene

Kemble, J. P. — Played Leontes in 1802 revival

Macready, William Charles — Played Leontes; famed for passionate statue scene

Faucit, Helen (Lady Martin) — Played Hermione in London, Dublin, Glasgow, Edinburgh

Kean, Charles — 1856 production at Princess's Theatre with classical costumes

Anderson, Mary (Mme. de Navarro) — Doubled Hermione and Perdita; 150 nights at Lyceum Theatre

Warner, Mrs. — Played Paulina opposite Lady Martin's Hermione

Winter, William — Critic; Shadows of the Stage (on Mary Anderson)

IV. Historical Sources, Chroniclers, and Dramatists

Greene, Robert — Author of Pandosto: The Triumph of Time, the direct source of the plot

Jonson, Ben — Earliest critic of 'sea-coast of Bohemia' (1619, Conversations with Drummond)

Drummond of Hawthornden — Recorded Jonson's conversations (1619)

Forman, Dr. Simon — Witnessed performance at the Globe, 15 May 1611

Herbert, Sir Henry — Master of the Revels; Office-book entry of 19 August 1623

Cunningham, Peter — Edited Extracts from the Accounts of the Revels (some records deemed forged)

Romano, Giulio — Painter/sculptor mentioned in the play; discussed by Elze

Caro, J. — Identified Lithuanian MS source as possible indirect source

Lope de Vega — El Marmol de Felisardo cited by Klein as having affinity with the play

Sidney, Sir Philip — Defence of Poesie (1595); pointed out dramatic improprieties

V. Other Persons Referenced

Furness, Horace Howard — Editor of this New Variorum Edition

Furness, H. H., Jr. — Copyright holder, 1926

Schmitz, L. Dora (Miss) — Translator of Elze's Essays on Shakespeare

Black, John — Translator of Schlegel's Lectures

Tieck, Dorothea — German translator

Tieck, Ludwig — German poet/critic; translator

Lessing, G. E. — First to sound Shakespeare's praises in Germany

Brandes, Georg — Danish critic; William Shakespeare (1896)

Swinburne, A. C. — A Study of Shakespeare

Tennyson, Alfred Lord — On three repartees in Shakespeare that bring tears

Ruskin, John — Munera Pulveris; on Shakespeare's naming practices

Dryden, John — Criticized The Winter's Tale for 'ridiculous incoherent story'

Voltaire — Cited by Johnson as example of 'slender criticism'

Adams, Sarah — Writer; witnessed Helen Faucit's performance; author of 'Nearer, my God, to Thee'

Ingram, J. K. — History of the Verse-Tests (New Shakspere Society)

Arber, Edward — Transcript of Stationers' Registers

Who are the New Variorum Critics?

Critics Cited in the New Variorum Edition of Twelfth Night

Edited by Horace Howard Furness

This document provides a comprehensive list of all critics, commentators, editors, scholars, translators, actors, and other persons cited in the New Variorum Edition of Shakespeare's Twelfth Night. Where the volume's "List of Books" identifies the specific work from which annotations are drawn, that bibliographic information is included.

I. Editors Collated in the Textual Notes

Editor Abbreviation Date(s)

III. Actors and Theatre Figures

Pepys, Samuel — Diarist; recorded seeing Twelfth Night in 1661, 1662–3, and 1668

Betterton, Mr. — Acted Sir Toby Belch in early Restoration performance

Harris, Mr. — Acted Sir Andrew Ague-cheek in early Restoration performance

Underhill, Mr. — Acted Fool in early Restoration performance

Lovel, Mr. — Acted Malvolio in early Restoration performance

Gibbs, Miss Ann — Acted Olivia in early Restoration performance

Heminges, John — Actor; presented Twelfth Night before James I, Easter Monday 1618

Condell, Henry — Actor; co-editor of the First Folio

Burbage, Richard — Great tragedian; may have created Malvolio

Burnaby, Charles — Author of Love Betray'd, 1703, partly taken from Twelfth Night

Bensley — Actor; played Malvolio with 'an air of Spanish loftiness,' praised by Lamb

Baddeley, Mr. — Actor; connected with Malvolio role

Parsons, Mr. — Actor; connected with Malvolio role

Kemble, John — Actor; occasionally played Malvolio

Dodd — Actor; praised by Lamb as an ideal Aguecheek

Lovegrove — Actor; revived character of Aguecheek

Phelps, Mr. — Actor; played Malvolio 'admirably'

Neilson, Miss — Actress; played Viola with 'saucy defiance'

Terry, Miss Ellen — Actress; played Viola with 'sweet and happy idiosyncrasy'

Rehan, Ada (Miss) — Actress; played Viola with 'wistful sadness' and 'lovely reserve'

Irving, Henry — Actor; played Malvolio at the Lyceum

Digges, Leonard — Wrote commendatory verses mentioning Malvolio's popularity

Macready — Actor-manager

Boocke, Robert L. — Drew costume illustrations for Shakespearian Costumes, London, 1889

IV. Historical Sources, Chroniclers, and Dramatists

Manningham, John — London barrister; his Diary records a performance of Twelfth Night, Feb. 2, 1601/2

Bandello, Matteo — Italian novelist; his Thirty-sixth Novel gives the story of Nicuola and Paolo

Belle-Forest, François de — French translator of Bandello's novel in Histoires Tragiques

Riche, Barnabe — Author of Apolonius and Silla (1581), primary English source

Secchi, Nicolo — Italian dramatist; author of Gl' Inganni (acted Milan, 1547)

Gonzaga, Curtio — Italian dramatist; author of another Gl' Inganni (Venice, 1592)

Gl' Intronati (The Thunderstruck) — Sienese Academy; authors of Gl' Ingannati (1531), the original source

Rueda, Lope de — Spanish dramatist; Los Enganos is a translation of Gl' Ingannati

Plautus — Roman dramatist; Amphitruo and Menaechmi cited as early source

Jones, Robert — Composer of Booke of Ayres (1601), containing 'Farewell, dear heart'

Morley, Thomas — Composer; his Consort Lessons (1599) contained tune of 'O Mistress mine'

Orsino, Virginio, Duke of Bracciano — Italian nobleman; ambassador to London winter 1600–1; possible source of Duke's name

Meres, Francis — Author of Wits Commonwealth (1598); did not mention Twelfth Night

Cinthio, Giraldi — Italian novelist; Hecatommithi cited in connection with source history

Hakluyt, Richard — Compiler of Voyages; the 'new Map' was published with his work

V. Other Persons Referenced

Furness, Horace Howard — Editor of this New Variorum Edition

Clark, W. G. — Co-editor of Cambridge Edition

Wright, W. Aldis — Co-editor of Cambridge Edition; sole editor of Second Edition (1891)

Reed, Isaac — Editor of Reed's Steevens Variorum editions

Boswell, James (the younger) — Editor of Boswell's Malone Variorum (1821)

Farmer, Dr. Richard — Scholar; cited on the learning of Shakespeare

Hunter, Joseph — Discovered identity of Manningham; traced Ingannati as source

Bright, B. H. — Literary friend of Hunter; connected with Manningham's Diary discovery

Gilchrist, Octavius — Of Stamford; pointed out Apolonius and Silla as source (1806)

Coote, C. H. — Scholar; maintained the 'new map' was published with Hakluyt's Voyages, 1599

Lenox, James — Founder of The Lenox Library; suggested identification of 'new Map' in 1860

Keller, Wolfgang — Co-author with Churchill of article on Latin MS Dramas

Marsh, J. F. — Noted allusion to Moll Cutpurse in Notes and Queries (1878)

White, Holt — Commentator; noted Pope's borrowing from Shakespeare

Gifford, William — Critic; dismissed Jonson's supposed allusion to Twelfth Night

Herder, J. G. V. — German translator of 'Come away, come away, death'

Dingelstedt, Franz — German translator of 'Come away, come away, death'

Kobell, L. von — German translator of 'Come away, come away, death' (Deutsche Revue, Berlin, 1892)

Laroche, Benjamin — French translator of 'Come away, come away, death' (Paris, 5th ed., 1869)

Schmitz, L. Dora — Translator of Ulrici's Shakespeare's Dramatic Art (Bohn's edition)

Black, John — Translator of A. W. von Schlegel's Lectures

Payne, John — Translator of Bandello (Villon Society)

Pettie, G. — Translator of Guazzo's Ciuile Conuersation

Young, Bartholomew — Co-translator of Guazzo's Ciuile Conuersation

Way, Albert — Editor of Promptorium Parvulorum (Camden Society)

Tieck, Ludwig — German author; Der junge Tischlermeister describes a performance of Twelfth Night

Bolin, Wilhelm — German critic; described staging in four acts (Jahrbuch, 1889)

Bahlsen, Dr. — Detected similarity between Twelfth Night and La Española en Florencia

Jonson, Ben — Dramatist; Every Man Out of His Humour cited for possible allusion

Marston, John — Dramatist; What You Will (1602) and Malcontent cited

Dekker, Thomas — Dramatist; co-author of Westward Ho (1605)

Webster, John — Dramatist; co-author of Westward Ho

Chapman, George — Dramatist; Sir Giles Goosecap (1601) cited

Daniel, Samuel — Poet; Sonnet V in Delia cited as source for Acteon allusion

Sidney, Sir Philip — Author of Arcadia; cited for parallels

Calderón — Spanish dramatist; La Española en Florencia ascribed to him

Lope de Vega — Spanish dramatist; La Española ascribed to him under title Burlas Veras

Rabelais — French satirist; Swinburne traces his influence on Twelfth Night

Molière — French dramatist; compared to Shakespeare by Montégut

Goethe — German poet; his saying about Shakespeare's central ideas quoted by Kreyssig

Herbert, Sir — Keeper of the Revels; his Diary records 'Malvolio' performed at court, 1622–3

Raleigh, Sir Walter — His trial (1603) cited for the 'thou' usage parallel

Coke, Sir Edward — Attorney General at Raleigh's trial; his 'thou' usage cited

Swinburne, A. C. — Critic; A Study of Shakespeare (1880)

Schmidt, Alexander — Author of Shakespeare-Lexicon

About This Project
Shakespeare Digital Variorum combines centuries of scholarly commentary with AI analysis. All Shakespeare texts are public domain and based on the Folger Shakespeare Library editions.
